llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper establishes fundamental high-dimensional limitations (вҖңcurse of dimensionalityвҖқ) of randomized smoothing for certifiable robustness against в„“p-bounded adversaries when p > 2. Specifically: (1) For a broad class of i.i.d. symmetric, zero-mean, continuous-support noise distributions with variance Пғ2, the largest certifiable в„“p radius r*p decays as O(1/d^{1/2вҲ’1/p}); Theorem 1 gives r*p upper bounds depending on p1(x), p2(x). (2) For generalized Gaussian smoothing (includes Gaussian, Laplace, uniform), a tighter bound r*p вүӨ 2Пғ d^{вҲ’(1/2вҲ’1/p)}(вҲҡlog(1/(1вҲ’p1)) + вҲҡlog(1/p2)) is proved (Theorem 2), showing Gaussian smoothing is optimal up to a constant factor for p вүҘ 2. (3) For uniform в„“вҲһ smoothing, r*p < 2b d^{вҲ’(1вҲ’1/p)} = 2вҲҡ3 Пғ d^{вҲ’(1вҲ’1/p)} (Theorem 4). (4) For uniform в„“1 smoothing, r*p < 2b/d, independent of p (Theorem 5). (5) They show Cohen et al.вҖҷs Gaussian certificate for в„“2 extends to в„“p (p вүҘ 2) via norm equivalence with the same d-scaling as their upper bounds, essentially matching up to constants. Empirical results on CIFAR-10 validate that changing the noise shape (for fixed Пғ) yields at best constant-factor gains and p1(x) is largely determined by Пғ, not the noise family.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: - Theoretical framework: Define smoothed classifier hВҜ(x) via additive noise О” вҲј P and study the largest certifiable в„“p radius r*p that guarantees label invariance. Construct adversarial classifiers and point pairs (x, xвҖІ) to upper bound r*p by вҲҘxвҲ’xвҖІвҲҘp. - i.i.d. smoothing (Theorem 1): Let S = вҲ‘ Zi, apply Chebyshev bounds to tail probabilities P(S вүҘ s) to bound thresholds s1, s2 linked to p1, p2, then propagate through the slab-separation argument to upper bound r*p, revealing O(1/d^{1/2вҲ’1/p}) decay. - Generalized Gaussian smoothing (Theorem 2): Sharpen analysis using Chernoff bounds and bounding E[e^{tZ}] via series expansion and Gamma function inequalities to obtain logarithmic dependence on 1/(1вҲ’p1) and 1/p2 with the same d-scaling. - Uniform в„“вҲһ smoothing (Theorem 4): Geometric overlap of shifted hypercubes; compare volumes V1вҲ’V2 and V2вҲ’V1 to bound the change in class probabilities after a shift Пө and derive r*p < 2b d^{вҲ’(1вҲ’1/p)}. - Uniform в„“1 smoothing (Theorem 5): Use в„“1-ball intersection geometry (Lemma 6) and volume formulas 2^d R^d/d! to bound overlap under a shift, yielding r*p < 2b/d. - Compare to Gaussian certificate (Cohen et al., 2019) extended to в„“p via norm equivalence: rp = Пғ d^{вҲ’(1/2вҲ’1/p)}(ОҰ^{-1}(p1) вҲ’ ОҰ^{-1}(p2))/2, matching d-dependence. - Empirical evaluation: Implement randomized smoothing with generalized Gaussian noise; estimate p1, p2 via Monte Carlo; compare achieved Gaussian certificates to the new upper bounds.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: - Datasets: CIFAR-10 at resolutions 32Г—32, 16Г—16, and 8Г—8; ImageNet statistics (median certificates) referenced from prior work for comparison. - Models: ResNet-110 trained for 90 epochs per configuration; identical architecture across resolutions (downscale image, add noise, then upsample to 32Г—32 before classification). - Noise distributions: Generalized Gaussian with parameter q (including Gaussian q=2, Laplace q=1, and approximating uniform as qвҶ’вҲһ); experiments often set q=p to match в„“p; multiple Пғ tested (0.12, 0.25, 0.50, 1.00) depending on resolution. - Certification protocol: Following Cohen et al. (2019) with 99.9% confidence; candidate class via small sample; then 100,000 samples to estimate p1(x); set p2(x)=1вҲ’p1(x). Compare actual Gaussian certificates to theoretical upper bounds derived here. - Observations: For fixed Пғ, p1(x) is largely invariant to the choice of i.i.d. noise distribution shape; at low Пғ and higher resolution, generalized Gaussian bounds can be tighter than i.i.d. bounds; at higher Пғ the i.i.d. bound is tighter. Scaling experiments show that in realistic high-dimensional tasks (e.g., ImageNet vs CIFAR-10), increased dimensionality does not sufficiently raise p1(x) to offset the d-scaling penalty.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: - Theoretical results focus primarily on i.i.d., symmetric, zero-mean, continuous-support smoothing distributions (with generalized Gaussian and specific uniform cases); findings may not extend to non-i.i.d. or input-dependent noise. - Bounds are most meaningful for p вүҘ 2; some become weak for p < 2 (and for p < 1 in в„“вҲһ uniform case). - Assumptions like e^{вҲ’d/4} < p2 вүӨ p1 < 1вҲ’e^{вҲ’d/4} are required in Theorem 2. - Upper bounds are derived via Chebyshev/Chernoff and may have conservative constants; tightness is shown only up to constant factors. - Empirical conclusions rely on specific architectures (ResNet-110) and datasets; no exploration of non-additive or data-dependent smoothing. - No new certifiers are proposed; analysis focuses on limits rather than constructive improvements.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: - Develop non-i.i.d., input-dependent, or data-adaptive smoothing schemes to escape the O(1/d^{1/2вҲ’1/p}) scaling for p > 2. - Explore correlated or structured noise aligned with data manifolds or model gradients to improve certifiable radii. - Design certification methods that exploit more information than just (p1, p2), potentially leveraging full class probability vectors or local decision geometry. - Investigate anisotropic or learned covariance kernels (e.g., non-isotropic Gaussian, low-rank perturbations) and theoretical limits thereof. - Extend analysis to other metrics (e.g., Wasserstein, perceptual distances) or non-additive perturbation models (patches, sparse attacks) in high dimensions. - Tighter inequalities and matching lower/upper bounds with optimal constants across broader distribution classes. - Combine adversarial training with advanced smoothing for p > 2 and study sample efficiency and estimator variance effects on certificates.

research_study_list.title: Randomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes
research_study_list.abstract:
Randomized smoothing is the current state-of-the-art defense with provable
robustness against $\ell_2$ adversarial attacks. Many works have devised new
randomized smoothing schemes for other metrics, such as $\ell_1$ or
$\ell_\infty$; however, substantial effort was needed to derive such new
guarantees. This begs the question: can we find a general theory for randomized
smoothing?
  We propose a novel framework for devising and analyzing randomized smoothing
schemes, and validate its effectiveness in practice. Our theoretical
contributions are: (1) we show that for an appropriate notion of "optimal", the
optimal smoothing distributions for any "nice" norms have level sets given by
the norm's *Wulff Crystal*; (2) we propose two novel and complementary methods
for deriving provably robust radii for any smoothing distribution; and, (3) we
show fundamental limits to current randomized smoothing techniques via the
theory of *Banach space cotypes*. By combining (1) and (2), we significantly
improve the state-of-the-art certified accuracy in $\ell_1$ on standard
datasets. Meanwhile, we show using (3) that with only label statistics under
random input perturbations, randomized smoothing cannot achieve nontrivial
certified accuracy against perturbations of $\ell_p$-norm $\Omega(\min(1,
d^{\frac{1}{p} - \frac{1}{2}}))$, when the input dimension $d$ is large. We
provide code in github.com/tonyduan/rs4a.
research_study_list.full_text: Randomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes Greg Yang* 1 Tony Duan* 1 2 J. Edward Hu 1 2 Hadi Salman 1 Ilya Razenshteyn 1 Jerry Li 1 Abstract Randomized smoothing is the current state-of-the- art defense with provable robustness against в„“2 adversarial attacks. Many works have devised new randomized smoothing schemes for other metrics, such as в„“1 or в„“вҲһ; however, substantial effort was needed to derive such new guarantees. This begs the question: can we п¬Ғnd a general theory for randomized smoothing? We propose a novel framework for devising and analyzing randomized smoothing schemes, and validate its effectiveness in practice. Our theoreti- cal contributions are: (1) we show that for an ap- propriate notion of вҖңoptimalвҖқ, the optimal smooth- ing distributions for any вҖңniceвҖқ norms have level sets given by the normвҖҷsWulff Crystal; (2) we pro- pose two novel and complementary methods for deriving provably robust radii for any smoothing distribution; and, (3) we show fundamental lim- its to current randomized smoothing techniques via the theory of Banach space cotypes. By com- bining (1) and (2), we signiп¬Ғcantly improve the state-of-the-art certiп¬Ғed accuracy in в„“1 on stan- dard datasets. Meanwhile, we show using (3) that with only label statistics under random in- put perturbations, randomized smoothing cannot achieve nontrivial certiп¬Ғed accuracy against per- turbations of в„“p-norm в„Ұ(min(1,d 1 pвҲ’1 2 )), when the input dimension dis large. We provide code in github.com/tonyduan/rs4a. 1. Introduction Deep learning models are vulnerable to adversarial exam- ples вҖ“ small imperceptible perturbations to their inputs that lead to misclassiп¬Ғcation (Goodfellow et al., 2015; Szegedy et al., 2014). To solve this problem, recent works proposed heuristic defenses that are robust to speciп¬Ғc classes of per- *Equal contribution 1Microsoft Research AI 2Work done as part of the Microsoft AI Residency Program. Corre- spondence to: Greg Yang <gregyang@microsoft.com>, Tony Duan <tony.duan@microsoft.com>, Jerry Li <jerrl@microsoft.com>. Proceedings of the 37 th International Conference on Machine Learning, Vienna, Austria, PMLR 119, 2020. Copyright 2020 by the author(s). turbations, but many would later be broken by stronger at- tacking algorithms (Carlini & Wagner, 2017; Athalye et al., 2018; Uesato et al., 2018). This led the community to both strengthen empirical defenses (Kurakin et al., 2016; Madry et al., 2017) as well as build certiп¬Ғed defenses that provide robustness guarantees, i.e., models whose predictions are constant within a neighborhood of their inputs (Wong & Kolter, 2018; Raghunathan et al., 2018a). In particular, ran- domized smoothing is a recent method that has achieved state-of-the-art provable robustness (Lecuyer et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019; Cohen et al., 2019). In short, given an input, it outputs the class most likely to be returned by a base classiп¬Ғer, typically a neural network, under random noise perturbation of the input. This mechanism confers stabil- ity of the output against в„“p perturbations, even if the base classiп¬Ғer itself is highly non-Lipschitz. Canonically, this noise has been Gaussian, and the adversarial perturbation it protects against has been в„“2 (Cohen et al., 2019; Salman et al., 2019a; Zhai et al., 2020), but some have explored other kinds of noises and adversaries as well (Lecuyer et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019; Dvijotham et al., 2019). In this paper, we seek to comprehensively understand the interaction between the choice of smoothing distribution and the perturbation norm.1 1. We propose two new methods to compute robust cer- tiп¬Ғcates for additive randomized smoothing against different norms. 2. We show that, for в„“1,в„“2,в„“вҲһadversaries, the optimal smoothing distributions have level sets that are their re- spective Wulff CrystalsвҖ” a kind of equilibrated crystal structure studied in physics since 1901 (Wulff). 3. Using the above advances, we obtain state-of-the-artв„“1 certiп¬Ғed accuracy on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. With stability training (Li et al., 2019), semi-supervised learning (Carmon et al., 2019), and pre-training in the fashion of Hendrycks et al. (2019), we further improve CIFAR-10 certiп¬Ғed accuracies, with >30% advantage over prior SOTA forв„“1 radius вүҘ1.5. See Table 1. 4. Finally, we leverage the classical theory of Banach space cotypes (Wojtaszczyk, 1996) to show that current techniques for randomized smoothing cannot certify nontrivial accuracy at more than в„Ұ(min(1,d 1 pвҲ’1 2 )) в„“p- radius, if all one uses are the probabilities of labels when classifying randomly perturbed input. 1V2 update: we added results using stability training, semi- supervised learning, and ImageNet pre-training. See Table 1. arXiv:2002.08118v5  [cs.LG]  23 Jul 2020Randomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes ImageNet в„“1 Radius 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 3.5 4.0 Laplace, Teng et al. (2019) (%) 48 40 31 26 22 19 17 14 Uniform, Ours (%) 55 49 46 42 37 33 28 25 + Stability Training 60 55 51 48 45 43 41 39 CIFAR-10 в„“1 Radius 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 3.5 4.0 Laplace, Teng et al. (2019) (%) 61 39 24 16 11 7 4 3 Uniform, Ours (%) 70 59 51 43 33 27 22 18 + Stability Training 70 60 53 47 43 39 35 28 + Stability Training, Semi-supervision 74 63 54 48 43 38 34 31 + Stability Training, Pre-training 74 62 55 48 43 40 37 33 Table 1.Certiп¬Ғed top-1 accuracies of our в„“1-robust classiп¬Ғers, vs previous state-of-the-art, at various radii, for ImageNet and CIFAR-10.3 2. Related Works Defences against adversarial examples are mainly divided into empirical defenses and certiп¬Ғed defenses. Empirical defenses are heuristics designed to make learned models empirically robust. An example of these are adver- sarial training based defenses (Kurakin et al., 2016; Madry et al., 2017) which optimize the parameters of a model by minimizing the worst-case loss over a neighborhood around the input to these models (Carlini & Wagner, 2017; Laidlaw & Feizi, 2019; Wong et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2020). Such defenses may seem powerful, but have no guarantees that they are not вҖңbreakableвҖқ. In fact, the majority of the empiri- cal defenses proposed in the literature were later вҖңbrokenвҖқ by stronger attacks (Carlini & Wagner, 2017; Athalye et al., 2018; Uesato et al., 2018; Athalye & Carlini, 2018). Certiп¬Ғed defenses guarantee that for any input x, the clas- siп¬ҒerвҖҷs output is constant within a small neighborhood ofx. Such defenses are typically based on certiп¬Ғcation methods that are either exact or conservative. Exact methods include those based on Satisп¬Ғability Modulo Theories solvers (Katz et al., 2017; Ehlers, 2017) or mixed integer linear program- ming (Tjeng et al., 2019; Lomuscio & Maganti, 2017; Fis- chetti & Jo, 2017), which, although guaranteed to п¬Ғnd adver- sarial examples if they exist, are unfortunately computation- ally inefп¬Ғcient. On the other hand, conservative methods are more computationally efп¬Ғcient, but might mistakenly п¬Ӯag a вҖңsafeвҖқ data point as vulnerable to adversarial examples (Wong & Kolter, 2018; Wang et al., 2018a;b; Raghunathan et al., 2018a;b; Wong et al., 2018; Dvijotham et al., 2018b;a; Croce et al., 2018; Salman et al., 2019b; Gehr et al., 2018; Mirman et al., 2018; Singh et al., 2018; Gowal et al., 2018; Weng et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018). However, none of these defenses scale to practical networks. Recently, a new method called randomized smoothing has been proposed as a probabilistically certiп¬Ғed defense, whose architecture- independence makes it scalable. 3Unless stated otherwise, these models were trained with noise augmentation. In our replication of Teng et al. (2019), our noise augmentation results matched their adversarial training results. Randomized smoothing Randomized smoothing was п¬Ғrst proposed as a heuristic defense without any guaran- tees (Liu et al., 2018; Cao & Gong, 2017). Later on, Lecuyer et al. (2018) proved a robustness guarantee for smoothed classiп¬Ғers from a differential privacy perspective. Subse- quently, Li et al. (2019) gave a stronger robustness guarantee utilizing tools from information theory. Recently, Cohen et al. (2019) provided a tight в„“2 robustness guarantee for randomized smoothing, applied by Salman et al. (2020) to provably defend pre-trained models for the п¬Ғrst time. Furthermore, a series of papers came out recently that devel- oped robustness guarantees against other adversaries such as в„“1-bounded (Teng et al., 2019), в„“вҲһ-bounded (Zhang* et al., 2020), в„“0-bounded (Levine & Feizi, 2019a; Lee et al., 2019), and Wasserstein attacks (Levine & Feizi, 2019b). In Section 4.3, we give a more in-depth comparison on how our techniques compare to their results. Wulff Crystal We are the п¬Ғrst to relate to adversarial ro- bustness the theory of Wulff Crystals. Just as the round soap bubble minimizes surface tension for a given volume, the Wulff Crystal minimizes certain similar surface energy that arises when the crystal interfaces with another material. The Russian physicist George Wulff п¬Ғrst proposed this shape via physical arguments in 1901 (Wulff, 1901), but its energy minimization property was not proven in full generality un- til relatively recently, building on a century worth of work (Gibbs, 1875; Wulff, 1901; Hilton, 1903; Liebmann, 1914; von Laue; Dinghas, 1944; Burton et al., 1951; Herring; Con- stable, 1968; Taylor, 1975; 1978; Fonseca & MВЁuller, 1991; Brothers & Morgan, 1994; Cerf, 2006). No-go theorems for randomized smoothing Prior to the initial submission of this manuscript, the only other no- go theorem for randomized smoothing in the context of adversarial robustness is Zheng et al. (2020). However, they are only concerned with a non-standard notion of certiп¬Ғed robustness that does not imply anything for the original problem. Moreover, they show that, under this different notion of robustness, if they are robust for в„“вҲһ, then the в„“2 norm of the noise must be large on average. While thisRandomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes provides indirect evidence for the hardness of certifying в„“вҲһ, it does not actually address the question. Our result, on the other hand, directly rules out a large suite of current techniques for deriving robust certiп¬Ғcates for all в„“p norms for p> 2, for the standard notion of certiп¬Ғed robustness. After the initial submission of this manuscript, we became aware of two concurrent works (Blum et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2020) that claim impossibility results for random- ized smoothing. Blum et al. (2020) demonstrate that, under some mild conditions, any smoothing distribution for в„“p with p >2 must have large component-wise magnitude. This gives indirect evidence for the hardness of the problem, but does not directly show a limit for the utility of random- ized smoothness for the robust classiп¬Ғcation problem, which we do in this work. Kumar et al. (2020) demonstrate that certain classes of smoothing distributions cannot certify в„“вҲһ without losing dimension-dependent factors. Our result is more general, as it rules out any class of smoothing distri- butions, and in fact, any smoothing scheme that allows the distribution to vary arbitrarily with the input point. 3. Randomized Smoothing Consider a classiп¬Ғer f from Rd to classes Yand a distribu- tion qon Rd. Randomized smoothing with qis a method that constructs a new, smoothed classiп¬Ғer gfrom the base classiп¬Ғer f. The smoothed classiп¬Ғer g assigns to a query point xthe class which is most likely to be returned by the base classiп¬Ғer f when x is perturbed by a random noise sampled from q, i.e., g(x) def = argmax cвҲҲY q(Uc вҲ’x) (1) where Uc is the decision region {xвҖІ вҲҲRd : f(xвҖІ) = c}, UcвҲ’xdenotes the translation of Uc by вҲ’x, and q(U) is the measure of U under q, i.e. q(U) = PОҙвҲјq(ОҙвҲҲU). Robustness guarantee for smoothed classiп¬Ғers For pвҲҲ [0,1],v вҲҲRd,deп¬Ғne the growth function Gq(p,v) def = sup UвҠҶRd:q(U)=p q(U вҲ’v), (2) One can think of U has the decision region of some base classiп¬Ғer. Thus Gq(p,v) gives the maximal growth of mea- sure of a set (i.e. decision region) when qis shifted by the vector v, if we only know the initial measure pof the set. Consider an adversary that can perturb an input additively by any vector vinside an allowed set B. In the case when Bis the в„“2 ball and qis the Gaussian measure, Cohen et al. (2019) gave a simple expression for Gq involving the Gaussian CDF, derived via the Neyman-Pearson lemma, which is later rederived by Salman et al. (2019a) as a nonlinear Lipschitz property. Likewise, the expression for Laplace distributions was derived by Teng et al. (2019). (See Theorem F.10 and Theorem F.11 for their expressions.) Suppose when the base classiп¬Ғer f classiп¬Ғes x+ Оҙ, ОҙвҲјq, the class cвҲҲY is returned with probabilityПҒ= PОҙвҲјq(f(x+ Оҙ) = c) > 1/2. Then the smoothed classiп¬Ғer g will not change its prediction under the adversaryвҖҷs perturbations if4 sup vвҲҲB Gq(1 вҲ’ПҒ,v) <1/2. (3) 4. Methods for Deriving Robust Radii Let qbe a distribution with a density function, and we shall write q(x),x вҲҲRd, for the value of the density function on x. Then, given a shift vector v вҲҲRd and a ratio Оә> 0, deп¬Ғne the Neyman-Pearson set NPОә def = {xвҲҲRd : Оәq(xвҲ’v) вүҘq(x)}. (4) Then the Neyman-Pearson lemma tells us that (Neyman & Pearson, 1933; Cohen et al., 2019) Gq(q(NPОә),v) = q(NPОә вҲ’v). (NP) While this gives way to a simple expression for the growth function when qis Gaussian (Cohen et al., 2019), it is difп¬Ғ- cult for more general distributions as the geometry of NPОә becomes hard to grasp. To overcome this difп¬Ғculty, we propose the level set method that decomposes this geome- try so as to compute the growth function exactly, and the differential method that upper bounds the growth function derivative, loosely speaking. 4.1. The Level Set Method For each t> 0, let Ut be the superlevel set Ut def = {xвҲҲRd : q(x) вүҘt}. Then its boundary вҲӮUt is the level set with q(x) = tunder regularity assumptions. The integral of qвҖҷs density is of course 1, but this integral can be expressed as the integral of the volumes of its superlevel sets: 1 = вҲ« q(x) dx= вҲ« вҲһ 0 Vol(Ut) dt. (вҖў) If qhas a differentiable density, then we may rewrite this as an integral of level sets (Theorem E.3): 1 = вҲ« вҲһ 0 вҲ« вҲӮUt t вҲҘвҲҮq(x)вҲҘ2 dxdt. In general, this yields an upper bound of the robust radius sup { r: sup вҲҘvвҲҘpвүӨr Gq(1 вҲ’ПҒ,v) <1/2 } вүӨsup {r: Gq(1 вҲ’ПҒ,ru) <1/2} for any particular uwith вҲҘuвҲҘp = 1. With sufп¬Ғcient symme- try, e.g. with в„“2 adversary and distributions with spherical level sets, this upper bound becomes tight for well-chosen u, and we can build a lookup table of certiп¬Ғed radii. The Differential Method To derive certiп¬Ғcation (robust radius lower bounds ) for more general distributions, we propose adifferential method, which can be thought of as a vast generalization of the proof in Salman et al. (2019a) of the Gaussian robust radius. The idea is to compute the largest possible inп¬Ғnitesimal increase in q-measure due to an inп¬Ғnitesimal adversarial perturba- tion. More precisely, given a norm вҲҘВ·вҲҘ, and a smoothing measure q, we deп¬Ғne ОҰ(p) def = sup вҲҘvвҲҘ=1 sup UвҠҶRd:q(U)=p lim rвҶҳ0 q(U вҲ’rv) вҲ’p r . (5) Intuitively, one can then think of 1/ОҰ(p) as the smallest possible perturbation in вҲҘВ·вҲҘ needed to effect a unit of inп¬Ғnitesimal increase in p. Therefore, Theorem 4.1 (Theorem F.6). The robust radius in вҲҘВ·вҲҘ is at least R def = вҲ« 1/2 1вҲ’ПҒ 1 ОҰ(p) dp, where ПҒis the probability that the base classiп¬Ғer predicts the right label under random perturbation by q. Example 4.4 (Appendices I.2.1 and I.3.1) . Facing an в„“1 adversary, cubical distributions, like that in Example 4.2, typically enjoy, via the differential method,в„“1 robust radii of the form R= c(ПҒвҲ’1/2) for some constant cdepending on the distribution. In general, the level set method always gives certiп¬Ғcate as tight as Neyman-Pearson, while the differential method is tight only for inп¬Ғnitesimal perturbations, but can be shown to be tight for certain families, like in Example 4.3 above. On the other hand, the latter will often give efп¬Ғciently evalu- able symbolic expressions and apply to more general distri- butions, while the former in general will only yield a table of robust radii, and only for distributions whose level sets are sufп¬Ғciently symmetric (such as a sphere or cube). For distributions that are covered by both methods, we com- pare the bounds obtained and note that the differential and level set methods yield almost identical robustness certiп¬Ғ- cates in high dimensions (e.g. number of pixels in CIFAR- 10 or ImageNet images). See Appendix B.1. Many earlier works used differential privacy orf-divergence methods to compute robust radii of smoothed modelsRandomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes (Lecuyer et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019; Dvijotham et al., 2019). In particular, Dvijotham et al. (2019) proposed a general f-divergence framework that subsumed all such works. Our robust radii are computed only from ПҒ; Dvi- jotham et al. called this the вҖңinformation-limitedвҖқ setting, and we shall compare with their robustness guarantees of this type. While their algorithm in a certain limit becomes as good as Neyman-Pearson, in practice outside the Gaussian distribution, their robust radii are too loose. This is evi- dent by comparing our baseline Laplace results in Table 1 with theirs, which are trained the same way. Additionally, our differential method often yields symbolic expressions for robust radii, making the certiп¬Ғcation algorithm easy to implement, verify, and run. Moreover, we derive robust- ness guarantees for many more (distributions, adversary) pairs (Fig. 1 and Table A.1). See Appendix B.2 for a more detailed comparison. 5. No-Go Results for Randomized Smoothing Recall that given a smoothing distributionq, a point xвҲҲRd, and a binary base classiп¬Ғer U вҠҶRd (identiп¬Ғed wth its deci- sion region), the smoothed classiп¬Ғer outputs sgn(ПҒвҲ’1/2) where ПҒ= q(U вҲ’x) is the вҖңconп¬ҒdenceвҖқ of this prediction (Eq. (1)). Randomized smoothing (via Neyman-Pearson) tells us that, if ПҒis large enough, then, no matter what U is, a small perturbation of xcannot decrease ПҒtoo much to change sgn(ПҒвҲ’1/2) (Eq. (3)). If all we care about is robustness, then the optimal strategy would set qto be an arbitrarily wide distribution (say, e.g. a wide Gaussian), and the resulting smoothed classiп¬Ғer is roughly constant. Of course, such a smoothed classiп¬Ғer can never achieve good clean accuracy, so it is not useful. Thus there is an inherent tension between 1) having to have large enough noise variance to be robust and 2) having to have small enough noise variance to avoid trivializing the smoothed classiп¬Ғer. In this section, we seek to formalize this tradeoff. As weвҖҷll show, even if we only assume a very weak condition on the accuracy, we can show strong upper bounds on the best robust radius for each в„“p norm. In fact, our negative results below will hold for a more gen- eral class of smoothing schemes than those in our positive results in previous sections: In what follows, a smoothing scheme for Rd is any family of probability distributions Q= {qx}xвҲҲRd. In practice, including in our paper, almost all smoothing schemes are translational, that is, there is some base distribution q, and for every x, the smoothing dis- tribution at xis deп¬Ғned by qx(U) = q(U вҲ’x), for all base classiп¬Ғers U вҠҶRd. The above discussion then motivates the following Deп¬Ғnition 7.1. Let вҲҘВ·вҲҘ be a norm over Rd, and let Q= {qx}xвҲҲRd be a smoothing scheme for Rd. We say that Q satisп¬Ғes (Оө,s,в„“ )-useful smoothing with respect to вҲҘВ·вҲҘ if: 1. ((Оө,s)-Robustness) For all x,y with вҲҘxвҲ’yвҲҘ< Оө, if U вҠҶRd is any set ( read: base classiп¬Ғer ) satisfying qx(U) вүҘ1/2 + s, then qy(U) вүҘ1/2. 2. (в„“-Accuracy) For all x,y with вҲҘxвҲ’yвҲҘ вүҘ1, there exists a set ( read: base classiп¬Ғer ) U вҠҶRd so that |qx(U) вҲ’qy(U)|вүҘ в„“. label prob. рқңҢ robust radius рқңҖ 0.5+рқ‘ 0.5 0 рқңҖ,рқ‘  -Robustness рқ‘Ҙ рқ‘Ұ рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘һрқ‘Ҙ рқ‘һрқ‘Ұ рқ‘үрқ‘ңрқ‘ҷ вүҘ в„“ вүҘ 1 в„“-Accuracy We pause to interpret this deп¬Ғnition. Condition (1) indicates how large the certiп¬Ғed radii can be for a classiп¬Ғer at any given point x, if the smoothed classiп¬Ғer assigns likelihood at least 1/2 + s to it; i.e. (1/2 + s,Оө) is a point on the robust radii curve in the style of Fig. A.1. The goal of the smoothing scheme is to achieve the largest possible Оө, for every п¬Ғxed s. In particular, observe that for в„“2, Gaussian smoothing achieves dimension-independent Оө, for every п¬Ғxed choice of s(Theorem F.10). Condition (2) says that the resulting smoothing should not вҖңcollapseвҖқ points: in particular, if x,y are far in norm, then there should be some smoothed classiп¬Ғer that distinguishes them. We argue that this is a very mild assumption. For Con- dition (2) to be satisп¬Ғed, the U which distinguishes these two points can be completely arbitrary. Thus, if it is violated for в„“= o(1), the two distributions are indistinguishable by any statistical test in high dimension, implying the impossi- bility of classifying between xand yafter smoothing. We seek to show that, for constant s and l, any (Оө,s,в„“ )- useful smoothing scheme must have Оө= o(1) for a number of norms, including в„“вҲһ. This would imply that any smooth- ing scheme that satisfying our weak notion of accuracy can only certify a vanishingly small radius, even when the conп¬Ғ- dence of the classiп¬Ғer is strictly bounded away from 1/2 by a constant.Randomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes Randomized Smoothing as Metric Embedding A smoothing scheme can be thought of as a mapping from a normed space supported on Rd to the space of distribu- tions, e.g. each point x is mapped to the distribution qx. We will show that Deп¬Ғnition 7.1 is roughly equivalent to a bi-Lipschitz condition on this mapping, where the target distributions are equipped with the total variation distance. Then the existence of a useful smoothing scheme is equiva- lent to whether (Rd,вҲҘВ·вҲҘ) can be embedded with low distor- tion into the total variation space of distributions. Classical mathematics has a deп¬Ғnitive answer to this question in the form of a geometric invariant, called the cotype. Deп¬Ғnition 7.2 (see e.g. Wojtaszczyk (1996)) . A normed space T = ( X,вҲҘВ·вҲҘ ) is said to have cotype p for 2 вүӨ p вүӨвҲһ if there exists C such that for all п¬Ғnite sequences x1,...,x n вҲҲX, we have E пЈ® пЈ° оө№оө№оө№оө№оө№оө№ nвҲ‘ j=1 Пғjxj оө№оө№оө№оө№оө№оө№ пЈ№ пЈ»вүҘCвҲ’1 пЈ« пЈӯ nвҲ‘ j=1 вҲҘxiвҲҘp пЈ¶ пЈё 1/p , where the Пғj are independent Rademacher random variables. The smallest such Cis denoted Cp(T). When the underlying space of the normed space T is Rd, JohnвҖҷs theorem (John, 1948) implies that any norm has co- type 2 with C2(T) вүӨO(d1/2). Because C2 lower bounds the distortion of a metric embedding of T, by the aforemen- tioned connection with randomized smothing,C2 also limits the usefulness of any smoothing scheme of T: Theorem 7.3. Let T be any normed space over Rd. There exist universal constants c,K > 0 so that any (Оө,s,в„“ )- useful smoothing scheme for T with s/в„“<c must have ОөвүӨK 4 вҲҡ s/в„“В·C2(T)вҲ’1. In particular, it is well-known that C2((Rd,вҲҘВ·вҲҘ p)) = в„Ұ(max(1,d1/2вҲ’1/p)), for all p вҲҲ[1,вҲһ]. Thus, as an im- mediately corollary, we get: Corollary 7.4. For the value of c in Theorem 7.3 and for p вҲҲ[1,вҲһ], any (Оө,s,в„“ )-useful smoothing scheme for (Rd,вҲҘВ·вҲҘp) with s/в„“<c must have ОөвүӨO(min(1,dвҲ’1/2+1/p)). It is easy to see that, up to constants, the Gaussian smoothing scheme achieves equality, and thus is optimal (in terms of dimension dependence), for all pвҲҲ[1,вҲһ]. Discussion After Cohen et al. (2019) showed the surpris- ing scalability of Gaussian randomized smoothing to high- dimensional в„“2-robust classiп¬Ғcation problems, many antic- ipated that this can be extended to в„“вҲһas well. One might also hope that, even though it seems like we cannot certify в„“2 radius that grows with input dimension, we could do so for в„“1. But Theorem 7.3 and Corollary 7.4 present a strong barrier to such hopes. In words: Without using more than the information of the probability ПҒof correctly classifying an input under random noise, no smoothing techniques can certify nontrivial robust accuracy at в„“вҲһ radius в„Ұ(dвҲ’1/2), or at в„“2 or в„“1 radius в„Ұ(1). Indeed, the в„“1-radii we can obtain nontrivial certiп¬Ғed ac- curacy at are on the same order between CIFAR10 and Imagenet (Fig. 2). However, there are some ways to bypass this barrier. For one, more information about the base classiп¬Ғer can be col- lected to produce better robustness certiп¬Ғcates. In fact, Dvi- jotham et al. (2019) proposed a вҖңfull-informationвҖқ algorithm that computes many moments of the base classiп¬Ғer in a convex optimization procedure to improve certiп¬Ғed radius, but it is 100 times slower than the вҖңinformation-limitedвҖқ algorithms we discuss here that use only ПҒ. It would be interesting to see whether this technique can be scaled up, and whether other methods can leverage more information7. Another route is to directly look for better randomized smoothing schemes for multi-class classiп¬Ғcation. We formu- lated our no-go result in the setting of binary classiп¬Ғcation, and it is not clear whether a similarly strong barrier applies for multi-class classiп¬Ғcation. However, current techniques for certiп¬Ғcation only look at the two most likely classes, and separately reason about how much each one can change by perturbing the input. Our no-go result then straightforwardly applies to this case as well. 8. Conclusion In this work, we have showed how far we can push ran- domized smoothing with different smoothing distributions against different в„“p adversaries, by presenting two new tech- niques for deriving robustness guarantees, by elucidating the geometry connecting the noise and the norm, and by empirically achieving state-of-the-art in в„“1 provable defense. At the same time, we have showed the limit current tech- niques face against в„“p adversaries when p >2, especially в„“вҲһ. Our results point out ways to bypass this barrier, by either leveraging more information about the base classiп¬Ғer or by taking advantage of the multi-class problem structure better. We wish to investigate both directions in the future. More broadly, randomized smoothing is a method for in- ducing stability in a mechanism while maintaining utility вҖ” precisely the bread and butter of differential privacy. We suspect our methods for deriving robustness guarantees here and for optimizing the noise distribution can be useful in that setting as well, where Laplace and Gaussian noise dom- inate the discussion. Whereas previous work Lecuyer et al. (2018) has applied differential privacy tools to randomized smoothing, we hope to go the other way around in the future. 7 Lee et al. (2019) also used the decision tree structure of their base classiп¬Ғer to improve в„“0 certiп¬Ғcation, but the в„“0-adversary does not fall within our framework.Randomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes Acknowledgements We thank Huan Zhang for brainstorming of ideas and per- forming a few experiments that unfortunately did not work out. Analysis of Robust Radii Here we make a few observations about the robust radii of the distributions studied in this paper. Distributions that concentrate around the same level set have similar robust radii. This is evident, for example, in the top middle subplot of Fig. A.1, where the distribution вҲқвҲҘxвҲҘвҲ’j вҲһeвҲ’вҲҘx/О»вҲҘвҲһ with вҖңsmallвҖқ j = 2972 has robust radii almost the same as those of вҲқeвҲ’вҲҘx/О»вҲҘвҲһ (here О»for each distribution is the one that setsПғ= 1), and both distributions concentrate around the sphere of radius вҲҡ d. We can also see this in the top right (в„“вҲһ-based power law), middle left (в„“1- based exponential law), bottom left (в„“2-based exponential law), and bottom middle (в„“2-basd power law) subplots of Fig. A.1. This is also reп¬Ӯected in the center subplot of Fig. A.1, which shows that Pareto distribution with large power gets the same robust radii as Laplace. The reason is that such a high-power Pareto distribution concentrates around an в„“1-ball in high dimension. We can understand this phenomenon intuitively via the level set method: Two distributions concentrating around the same level set will have Eq. ( вҲЁ) and Eq. ( вҲ§) evaluate to similar quantities. Among distributions concentrated around some level set, the shape of the level set is the biggest determinant of performance. This is evident in the top left, middle right, and bottom right subplots of Fig. A.1. Distributions that donвҖҷt concentrate on a level set do worse than those that do. This is evident, for example, in the top middle subplot of Fig. A.1, where the distribution вҲқвҲҘxвҲҘвҲ’j вҲһeвҲ’вҲҘx/О»вҲҘвҲһ with вҖңlargeвҖқj = 3070 has robust radii much smaller than those of вҲқeвҲ’вҲҘx/О»вҲҘвҲһ. Same thing can be observed in the top right, center, middle right, bottom left, bottom middle subplots of Fig. A.1. Introducing a singularity at the origin only reduces ro- bust radii. The top middle and bottom left subplots of Fig. A.1 illustrate this point. Thus, we see no evidence for the вҖңsoap-bubble hypothesisвҖқ put forth by Zhang* et al. (2020); see also Fig. C.8. Introducing a fatter tail yields larger robust radii for large ЛҶПҒlower, as long as the level set concentration is not affected. The middle left and bottom left subplots of Fig. A.1 demonstrate this behavior. The robust radii for- mulas for exp(вҲ’вҲҘxвҲҘвҲһ) (Theorem I.6) and for the uniform distribution (Theorem I.8) also reп¬Ӯect this, as the former has robust radius вҶ’вҲһ as ПҒвҶ’1, but the latter has a п¬Ғnite maximal robust radius. B.2. In-Depth Comparison with Dvijotham et al. (2019) The information-limited certiп¬Ғcation algorithm in Dvi- jotham et al. (2019) relaxes the optimization problem sup vвҲҲB Gq(p,v) = sup qвҖІвҲҲqB sup U:q(U)=p qвҖІ(U) вүӨ sup qвҖІвҲҲDF(q) sup U:q(U)=p qвҖІ(U) (7) enlarging the set of shifted distributions qB def = {q(В·вҲ’v) : v вҲҲB} to the set of distributions close to q in several f- divergences DF def = {qвҖІ : Df(qвҖІвҲҘq) вүӨПөf,вҲҖf вҲҲF}, for a set of functions F. Dvijotham et al. (2019) showed that when F consists of all Hockey-Stick divergences, Eq. (7) becomes tight,, but in practice this is not feasible. In fact, Dvijotham et al. (2019) admits themselves that It turns out that the Renyi and KL divergences are computationally attractive for a broad class of smoothing measures, while the Hockey-Stick di- vergences are theoretically attractive as they leadRandomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes to optimal certiп¬Ғcates in the information-limited setting. However, Hockey-Stick divergences are harder to estimate in general, so we only use them for Gaussian smoothing measures. Concretely, the looseness of their relaxation can be observed when comparing our baseline Laplace results (Table 1) with theirs. Operationally, their algorithm proceeds as follows 1. For each distribution qand function f, manually п¬Ғnd the f-divergence вҖңballвҖқ that contains {q(В·вҲ’v) : v вҲҲ B}, i.e. compute {Пөf}fвҲҲF such that {q(В·вҲ’v) : vвҲҲB}вҠҶ{ qвҖІ: Df(qвҖІвҲҘq) вүӨПөf,вҲҖf вҲҲF}. 2. Then they relax the original certiп¬Ғcation problem to the certiп¬Ғcation of all qвҖІclose to qin f-divergence, i.e. they solve Eq. (7) for the Пөf found in the previous step. The 2nd step is a straightforward low-dimensional convex optimization problem, but the trickiness of the 1st step limits the distributions they can apply their technique to. For example, they only know how to do step 1 for exp(вҲ’вҲҘxвҲҘp) against в„“padversary, but not againstв„“r for rМё= p; in contrast, our differential method computes robust radii for Laplace against в„“вҲһperturbation, for example. Does Training and Testing on Different Noises Help? One may hope that certifying with a different noise than what a model was trained on may improve performance of the classiп¬Ғer. For example, Exp2 noise with large j has more mass concentrated around zero compared to Gaussian noise, and may therefore be easier to вҖңde-noiseвҖқ. In this section we п¬Ғnd that training and testing with different noises does not improve clean accuracy, when we compare noises at a п¬Ғxed level of Пғ = 0.5 (Figure Fig. C.9). For all the noises we considered, testing a model with the same noise it was trained upon results in the best clean accuracy. This suggests the classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs de-noising process is quite reliant on the properties of the noise to which it is exposed in the training process.Randomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes Figure C.4.Effect of Architecture. Experiment Hyperparameters For all experiments we trained with a cosine-annealed learning rate of 0.1, opti- mized by stochastic gradient descent with momentum of 0.9 and weight decay of 0.0001. For ImageNet experiments we used a ResNet-50 model and trained with a batch size of 64 for 30 epochs. For CIFAR-10 experiments we used a Wide ResNet 40-2 model and trained with a batch size of 128 for 120 epochs. Ablation studies with a fully connected neural network em- ployed two hidden layers of 2048 and 512 nodes followed by ReLU activations, trained with a learning rate of 0.01. To compute the top categories for certiп¬Ғcation (which used N = 100,000 samples), we used 64 samples. Mathematical Preliminaries In this section, we rigorously deп¬Ғne several mathematical notions and their properties that will recurrent throughout what follows. We will be brief here, but readers can skip this on п¬Ғrst reading and refer back when necessary. Note about Notation We will use Vol to denote measure, typically Hausdorff measure with the dimension implicit from context. When integrating over a measurable set, the underlying measure is also typically the Hausdorff measure as well. By вҲӮU of a set U, we typically mean reduced boundary (when U has п¬Ғnite perimeter), especially in a measure-theoretic context. Readers needing more back- ground can consult Evans & Gariepy (2015). Sobolev Functions and Regular Functions While many distributions like Gaussian have continuously differentiable densities, many others, like Laplace, only have вҖңweakвҖқ derivatives. Thus, to cover all such distributions, we need to pin down a notion of вҖңweakly differentiable.вҖқ Deп¬Ғnition E.1. Let в„Ұ вҠҶRd, and f : в„Ұ вҶ’R, g: в„Ұ вҶ’Rd. We say g is a weak derivative of f if for every smooth function ПҶ: в„Ұ вҶ’Rd with compact support, вҲ« fdiv ПҶ= вҲ’ вҲ« gВ·ПҶ. We write g= вҲҮf in this case. For any open set в„Ұ вҠҶRd, the Sobolev space W1,p(в„Ұ) is deп¬Ғned as the functions f вҲҲLp(в„Ұ) whose weak derivative exists and is in Lp(в„Ұ; Rd), i.e. W1,p(в„Ұ) def = {f вҲҲLp(в„Ұ) : вҲҮf вҲҲLp(в„Ұ; Rd)}. Deп¬Ғnition E.2. Let в„Ұ вҠҶRdbe an open set. For the purpose of this paper, we say a function f : в„Ұ вҶ’R is regular if f вҲҲW1,1(в„Ұ). This means that f has a weak derivative вҲҮf such that both f and вҲҮf are integrable. For example, ReLU is not a continuously differentiable function, but it is regular since it has the Heavyside step function as its weak derivative. Coarea Formula and the Weak SardвҖҷs Theorem Theorem E.3 (Coarea Formula (Federer, 2014; Evans & Gariepy, 2015)). Let в„Ұ вҠҶRd be an open set, g вҲҲL1(в„Ұ), and f : Rd вҶ’R regular in the sense of Deп¬Ғnition E.2. Deп¬Ғne Ut def = {x : f(x) вүҘt}to be fвҖҷs superlevel sets. Then вҲ« в„Ұ g(x)вҲҘвҲҮf(x)вҲҘ2 dx= вҲ« R вҲ« вҲӮUt g(x) dxdt. Here the integral in x in the RHS is over the (d вҲ’1)- dimensional Hausdorff measure of the reduced boundary of Ut, which we abuse notation and denote as вҲӮUt. The regularity of f can be replaced by weaker conditions (see Evans & Gariepy (2015)), but the statement here suf- п¬Ғces for our purposes. By setting g in Theorem E.3 to be the indicator function over the set where the gradient of f vanishes, we get Theorem E.4 (Weak Sard). For any f : Rd вҶ’R regular in the sense of Deп¬Ғnition E.2, let Z def = {xвҲҲRd : вҲҮf(x) = 0}. Let Ut def = {x: f(x) вүҘt}denote the superlevel set of f at level t. Then Vol(ZвҲ©вҲӮUt) = 0Randomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes for almost every t вҲҲR. Here Vol denote the Hausdorff measure of dimension dвҲ’1, and again вҲӮUt denotes reduced boundary. Sobolev Functions and Absolute Continuity Recall the standard deп¬Ғnition of absolute continuity, which can be thought of as a more general notion of differentiability. Deп¬Ғnition E.5. A function f : R вҶ’R is called absolute continuous if there exists a Lebesgue integrable function g: R вҶ’R and some aвҲҲR, such that f(x) = f(a) + вҲ« x a g(t) dt. Such an f has derivative fвҖІ almost everywhere, and fвҖІ coincides with galmost everywhere. Sobolev functions are known to be absolutely continuous on every line, and this property roughly captures all Sobolev functions. Theorem E.6 (ACL Property of Sobolev Functions (Nikodym, 1933)). Let в„Ұ вҠҶRd be an open set. The follow- ing statements hold. вҖў Let f : в„Ұ вҶ’R be Sobolev, f вҲҲW1,p(в„Ұ). Then possi- bly after modifying f on a set of measure 0, for every uвҲҲRd, the function tвҶҰвҶ’f(x+ tu) is absolutely con- tinuous for almost every x. Furthermore, the (classical) directional derivative Duf is in Lp(в„Ұ) for every u. вҖў Conversely, if the restriction of a functionf : в„Ұ вҶ’R on almost every line parallel to the coordinate axes is absolutely continuous, then pointwise gradient вҲҮf exists almost everywhere, and f вҲҲW1,p(в„Ұ) as long as f,вҲҮf вҲҲLp(в„Ұ). Wulff Crystal The following is an intuitive statement of the main isoperi- metric property of Wulff Crystals. Theorem G.1 (Isoperimetric property of Wulff Crystals (Brothers & Morgan, 1994), informal statement). Let вҲҘВ·вҲҘ be any norm on Rn. Let Zbe the Wulff Crystal of вҲҘВ·вҲҘ, i.e. the unit ball of the norm вҲҘВ·вҲҘвҲ—dual to вҲҘВ·вҲҘ. Z = {x: вҲҘxвҲҘвҲ—вүӨ1}. Let в„Ұ be any measurable subset of Rn with п¬Ғnite perimeter and of the same volume as Z. Then with n(в„Ұ,x) denoting the normal vector at xwith respect to в„Ұ, normalized to have в„“2 norm 1, вҲ« вҲӮв„Ұ вҲҘn(в„Ұ,x)вҲҘdxвүҘ вҲ« вҲӮZ вҲҘn(Z,x)вҲҘdx with equality holding if and only ifв„Ұ differs from a translate of Zby a set of volume zero. This statement carries across the core essence of the isoperimetry, and is a rigorous statement if в„Ұ is restricted to have smooth boundary, but care needs to be taken to ex- plain the concept of вҖңп¬Ғnite perimeter,вҖқ вҖңnormal vector,вҖқ the вҖңboundary вҲӮв„Ұ,вҖқ and the boundary measure onвҲӮв„Ұ, in the con- text of general, measurable в„Ұ. These quantities are deп¬Ғned in Appendix H, but we also refer the interested reader to (Brothers & Morgan, 1994) for more mathematical details. G.1. Wulff Crystals are Zonotopes In this paper, the norm вҲҘВ·вҲҘ referred to in Theorem G.1 will take the form of вҲҘxвҲҘ= Ev|вҹЁx,vвҹ©|where vis sampled from some distribution, as in Deп¬Ғnition 5.1. When v is sampled uniformly from some п¬Ғnite set of vectors S, then the Wulff Crystal B = {x : вҲҘxвҲҘвҲ—вүӨ1}is proportional to the Zonotope of S(McMullen, 1971). Deп¬Ғnition G.2 (Zonotope). Given a п¬Ғnite collection of vec- tors S, the zonotope Zon(S) is deп¬Ғned as the Minkowski sum of the vectors of S, i.e. Zon(S) def = {вҲ‘ vвҲҲS avv: av вҲҲ[0,1],вҲҖvвҲҲS } . The zonotope can be viewed as a linear projection of the cube [0,1]Ssending each unit vector to a vector of S. Example G.3. If Bis the в„“1 unit ball, then Zon(Vert(B)) is a cube. If Bis the в„“вҲһunit ball, then in 2 dimensions, Zon(Vert(B)) is a rhombus; in 3 dimensions, it is rhombic dodecahedron; in higher dimensions, there is no simpler description of the resulting polytope. Proposition G.4. The Wulff Crystal w.r.t.Bis equal to the zonotope 2 |Vert(B)|Zon(Vert(B)). The volume of a zonotope, and thus of Wulff Crystals, can be computed easily using the following formula. Proposition G.5. Let Sbe a п¬Ғnite set of vectors in Rd. Then the d-dimensional volume of Zon(S) is given by вҲ‘ TвҠҶS:|T|=d |Vol(Zon(T))|= вҲ‘ TвҠҶS:|T|=d |det T|, where det T is the determinant of the square matrix with vectors of T as columns. G.2. Generalization of Differential Method and Wulff Crystal Optimality Results to Bounded Variation Densities While the regularity condition Deп¬Ғnition E.2 covers most distributions we care about, we still have to reason sepa- rately about, e.g. uniform distributions on sets, or mixture of such distributions and regular distribution. However, regu- larity can be weakened to the notion ofbounded variation to cover all such cases. Bounded variation (BV) is вҖңessentially the weakest measure theoretic sense in which a function can be differentiableвҖқ (Evans & Gariepy, 2015). BV functions include the usual continuously differentiable functions as well as indicator functions of вҖңп¬Ғnite perimeterвҖқ sets. More generally, the notion of BV allows a вҖңcontrolledвҖқ amount of jump-type discontinuities. Our differential method and our Wulff Crystal optimality results can be generalized to distributions with BV densities. Readers exposed to the notion of bounded variation for the п¬Ғrst time might п¬Ғnd it helpful to mentally substitute вҖңBVвҖқ in our results below with вҖңdifferentiableвҖқ or with вҖңindicator functionвҖқ on the п¬Ғrst read through. All probability distribu- tion densities we work with concretely in this paper have bounded variation. Deп¬Ғnition H.1 (Bounded Variation). Let в„Ұ вҠҶRd be an open set. A function f вҲҲL1(в„Ұ) is said be of bounded variation (or BV), writtenf вҲҲBV(в„Ұ), if there exists a п¬Ғnite Radon measure |Df|on Rd along with a vector function n : Rd вҶ’Rd with вҲҘn(x)вҲҘ2 = 1 almost everywhere w.r.t. |Df|, such that, for every compactly supported, continuous differentiable ПҶ: в„Ұ вҶ’Rd, we have вҲ« в„Ұ f(x) divПҶ(x) dx= вҲ’ вҲ« в„Ұ вҹЁПҶ,n(x)вҹ©|Df|(x). (23) We denote by Df the vector measure n|Df|. Example H.2. If u is differentiable, then Du(x) is just the vector measure вҲҮu(x) dxand |Du|(x) is вҲҘвҲҮu(x)вҲҘ2 dx, and Eq. (23) follows just by ordinary integration by parts. Same thing holds for Sobolev (i.e. weakly differentiable) functions. Example H.3. If uis the indicator function of, for exam- ple, a ball, then |Du|is the (dвҲ’1)-dimensional Hausdorff measure supported on its boundary (a sphere), and n(x) is the unit normal at xpointing inward. More generally, this characterization of Duas the boundary measure with unit normals holds when uis the indicator function of a set of п¬Ғnite perimeter. Deп¬Ғnition H.4 (Sets of Finite Perimeter). A set U has п¬Ғnite perimeter if its indicator function ПҮis a BV function. In this case, we write вҲ« вҲӮU g(x) dx def = вҲ« g(x)|DПҮ|(x) (24) for any Borel function g. For sufп¬Ғcently smooth sets U (like a sphere), the LHS of Eq. (24) can be interpreted as an integral over the Hausdorff measure of the topological boundary вҲӮU, and Eq. (24) still holds. More generally, there is a subset of the topological boundary, called the reduced boundary of U, containing вҖңalmost every pointвҖқ ofвҲӮU, such that the LHS of Eq. (24) can be interpreted as an integral over the Hausdorff measure of the reduced boundary. See (Evans & Gariepy, 2015) for more details. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Presents a general theory and practical framework for randomized smoothing across norms. (1) Introduces two general certification techniquesвҖ”the level set method (exact via coarea/NeymanвҖ“Pearson geometry) and the differential method (infinitesimal growth bound integrating 1/ОҰ)вҖ”that yield provable radii for broad families of smoothing distributions. (2) Proves that, for symmetric norm balls, optimal smoothing distributions (in an appropriate sense) have level sets equal to the normвҖҷs Wulff Crystal (e.g., cube for в„“1, sphere for в„“2, a zonotope for в„“вҲһ). (3) Derives certified radii for many distributionвҖ“norm pairs (Gaussian, Laplace, uniform, exponential/power-law families), producing closed forms or efficient tables. (4) Achieves state-of-the-art в„“1 certified accuracy on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet using uniform (cube) noise, further boosted by stability training, semi-supervision, and pre-training. (5) Establishes a no-go theorem: any information-limited randomized smoothing scheme (using only label probabilities under noise) cannot certify beyond O(min(1, d^{-1/2+1/p})) radius for в„“p (notably O(d^{-1/2}) for в„“вҲһ), via Banach space cotype/metric-embedding arguments.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Growth function and NeymanвҖ“Pearson sets: Formalizes certification via G_q(p, v), the worst-case measure growth under shifts, and uses NP sets to link to exact bounds for certain distributions.
- Level set method: Decomposes NP measures via the coarea formula into integrals over level/superlevel sets; enables exact or numerically precise computation when level sets are symmetric (spheres, cubes). Provides precomputation/lookup algorithms for radii (Algorithms 1вҖ“2).
- Differential method: Defines ОҰ(p) as the worst-case directional derivative of set measure under infinitesimal shift; integrates R = вҲ«_{1-ПҒ}^{1/2} 1/ОҰ(p) dp to lower-bound robust radius. Produces symbolic expressions for broad distribution families (iid log-concave/convex*, в„“вҲһ/в„“2-based exponentials, power law, Pareto), and recovers known Gaussian/Laplace bounds.
- Wulff Crystals: Uses geometric measure theory (isoperimetry for sets of finite perimeter, coarea, BV functions) to prove that among level-equivalent distributions, those with Wulff Crystal superlevel sets minimize ОҰ(1/2); and among uniform measures, Wulff Crystal-shaped supports minimize instantaneous measure growth. Provides zonotope characterization and examples (cube for в„“1, sphere for в„“2, zonotope of {Вұ1}^d for в„“вҲһ).
- No-go theorem: Models smoothing schemes as metric embeddings into total variation space, applies threshold-map arguments and cotype lower bounds (via embeddings into в„“_{0.99}), yielding dimension-dependent limits for information-limited randomized smoothing.
- Derived closed-form/efficient bounds: Table of radii for numerous distributions and norms; includes generalizations to BV densities and multiple families (e.g., Gamma/Beta/Beta-prime based formulas).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Datasets: CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. Additional data: 80 Million Tiny Images (pseudo-labeled) for semi-supervised training; downsampled ImageNet for pre-training.
- Models: Wide ResNet 40-2 (CIFAR-10), ResNet-50 (ImageNet). Also ablations with MLP and AlexNet.
- Training: Primarily noise augmentation with various smoothing distributions; additional setups include stability training (Оі=6), semi-supervised self-training with 500k pseudo-labeled images, and pre-training followed by fine-tuning.
- Distributions evaluated: Gaussian, Laplace, uniform over в„“вҲһ-cube, в„“вҲһ-based exponential/power-law families, Pareto (iid), в„“2-based exponential/power-law, uniform over в„“2-ball. Swept noise scale Пғ over a broad range; for cube-level-set distributions used larger Пғ range to cover large radii.
- Certification: Monte Carlo with N=100,000 samples and failure probability Оұ=0.001. Report certified top-1 accuracy vs radius; for each radius, select the best model across Пғ (upper envelope). Considered в„“1, в„“2, and в„“вҲһ adversaries using the new bounds/tables.
- Key findings: Uniform (cube) noise substantially improves в„“1 certified accuracy on both datasets, setting new SOTA; spherical-level-set noises are best for в„“вҲһ and comparable for в„“2. Code: github.com/tonyduan/rs4a.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- The strongest results and improvements are for в„“1; в„“2 results are comparable to Gaussian baselines, and в„“вҲһ remains limited by dimension-dependent barriers.
- The certification framework and no-go theorem focus on information-limited smoothing (using only label probabilities under noise); stronger certificates may require richer classifier statistics or structure.
- Level set method is most tractable for highly symmetric level sets (spheres/cubes); general shapes may require numerical tables or be hard to analyze.
- Optimal Wulff distributions for в„“вҲһ are complex (zonotopes of {Вұ1}^d) and difficult to sample; spherical approximations are used instead.
- Theoretical optimality results for Wulff Crystal distributions are clearest near hard inputs (ПҒвүҲ1/2); behavior far from this regime is not fully characterized.
- Certification requires large Monte Carlo budgets (N=100k) and can be computationally intensive; noise can degrade clean accuracy at higher Пғ.
- Assumptions include even/symmetric densities, BV regularity, and symmetry of the norm ball; results may not directly extend beyond these conditions.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Move beyond the information-limited setting: develop scalable full-information certificates that leverage more statistics (moments, logits, decision regions) or classifier structure to bypass cotype barriers.
- Multi-class specific certification using joint reasoning about more than the top-2 classes; design smoothing and analysis tailored to multi-class geometry.
- Practical constructions/samplers for в„“вҲһ Wulff Crystal (zonotope) distributions or tight spherical approximations with improved constants.
- Extend level set/differential methods to less symmetric level sets with efficient computation; automate symbolic derivations for broader distribution families.
- Integrate the new bounds into certified training objectives; combine with adversarial/stability training, semi-supervised learning, and pre-training systematically.
- Explore other threat models and metrics (e.g., Wasserstein, functional norms) under the same framework.
- Theoretically characterize optimality away from ПҒвүҲ1/2 and understand trade-offs between clean accuracy and robustness for different noise families.
- Bridge to differential privacy: transfer optimization and analysis of noise distributions between robustness and privacy objectives.

research_study_list.title: Higher-Order Certification For Randomized Smoothing
research_study_list.abstract:
Randomized smoothing is a recently proposed defense against adversarial
attacks that has achieved SOTA provable robustness against $\ell_2$
perturbations. A number of publications have extended the guarantees to other
metrics, such as $\ell_1$ or $\ell_\infty$, by using different smoothing
measures. Although the current framework has been shown to yield near-optimal
$\ell_p$ radii, the total safety region certified by the current framework can
be arbitrarily small compared to the optimal. In this work, we propose a
framework to improve the certified safety region for these smoothed classifiers
without changing the underlying smoothing scheme. The theoretical contributions
are as follows: 1) We generalize the certification for randomized smoothing by
reformulating certified radius calculation as a nested optimization problem
over a class of functions. 2) We provide a method to calculate the certified
safety region using $0^{th}$-order and $1^{st}$-order information for
Gaussian-smoothed classifiers. We also provide a framework that generalizes the
calculation for certification using higher-order information. 3) We design
efficient, high-confidence estimators for the relevant statistics of the
first-order information. Combining the theoretical contribution 2) and 3)
allows us to certify safety region that are significantly larger than the ones
provided by the current methods. On CIFAR10 and Imagenet datasets, the new
regions certified by our approach achieve significant improvements on general
$\ell_1$ certified radii and on the $\ell_2$ certified radii for color-space
attacks ($\ell_2$ restricted to 1 channel) while also achieving smaller
improvements on the general $\ell_2$ certified radii. Our framework can also
provide a way to circumvent the current impossibility results on achieving
higher magnitude of certified radii without requiring the use of data-dependent
smoothing techniques.
research_study_list.full_text: Higher-Order Certiп¬Ғcation for Randomized Smoothing Jeet Mohapatra1 Ching-Yun Ko1 Tsui-Wei Weng1,2 Pin-Yu Chen2 Sijia Liu2 Luca Daniel1 1 MIT 2 MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab, IBM Research Abstract Randomized smoothing is a recently proposed defense against adversarial attacks that has achieved state-of-the-art provable robustness against в„“2 perturbations. A number of publications have extended the guarantees to other metrics, such as в„“1 or в„“вҲһ, by using different smoothing measures. Although the current framework has been shown to yield near-optimal в„“p radii, the total safety region certiп¬Ғed by the current framework can be arbitrarily small compared to the optimal. In this work, we propose a framework to improve the certiп¬Ғed safety region for these smoothed classiп¬Ғers without changing the underlying smoothing scheme. The theoretical contributions are as follows: 1) We generalize the certiп¬Ғcation for randomized smoothing by reformulating certiп¬Ғed radius calculation as a nested optimization problem over a class of functions. 2) We provide a method to calcu- late the certiп¬Ғed safety region using zeroth-order and п¬Ғrst-order information for Gaussian-smoothed classiп¬Ғers. We also provide a framework that generalizes the calculation for certiп¬Ғcation using higher-order information. 3) We design efп¬Ғcient, high-conп¬Ғdence estimators for the relevant statistics of the п¬Ғrst-order information. Combining the theoretical contribution 2) and 3) allows us to certify safety region that are signiп¬Ғcantly larger than the ones provided by the current methods. On CIFAR10 and Imagenet datasets, the new regions certiп¬Ғed by our approach achieve signiп¬Ғcant improvements on general в„“1 certiп¬Ғed radii and on the в„“2 certiп¬Ғed radii for color-space attacks (в„“2 perturbation restricted to only one color/channel) while also achieving smaller improvements on the general в„“2 certiп¬Ғed radii. As discussed in the future works section, our framework can also provide a way to circumvent the current impossibility results on achieving higher magnitudes of certiп¬Ғed radii without requiring the use of data-dependent smoothing techniques. 1 Introduction Deep neural networks (DNNs) can be highly sensitive, i.e., small imperceptible input perturbations can lead to mis-classiп¬Ғcation [1, 2]. This poses a big problem for the deployment of DNNs in safety critical applications including aircraft control systems, video surveillance and self-driving cars, which require near-zero tolerance to lack of robustness. Thus, it is important to provide guarantees for the robustness of deep neural network models against multiple worst-case perturbations. Popular threat models are the в„“p-norm-based attacks, where possible perturbations are constrained in an в„“p-ball with respect to a given input x. To that end recent research efforts have focused on attack-agnostic robustness certiп¬Ғcation which, given an input x, provides a safety region within which the model is guaranteed not to change its prediction. Randomized smoothing is a recently-proposed defense [ 3, 4, 5] that has achieved state-of-the-art robustness guarantees. Given any classiп¬Ғer f, denoted as a base classiп¬Ғer, randomized smoothing Preprint. Under review. arXiv:2010.06651v1  [cs.LG]  13 Oct 2020predicts the class that is вҖңmost likelyвҖқ to be returned when noise is added to the input x. Thus, randomized smoothing acts as an operator that given a base classiп¬Ғer and a noise model, denoted as smoothing measure, produces a new classiп¬Ғer, denoted as the smoothed classiп¬Ғer. The smoothed classiп¬Ғers thus produced are easily-certiп¬Ғable with strong (even near-optimal for в„“2) robustness guarantees under various в„“p norm threat models [3, 6, 7]). However, for any given threat model, the state-of-the-art robustness guarantees are achieved only for the smoothed classiп¬Ғers obtained using very speciп¬Ғc smoothing measures. Thus, the classiп¬Ғers that attain state-of-the-art guarantees under one threat model might perform poorly under another (Gaussian-smoothed classiп¬Ғers are optimal under в„“2 but perform poorly under в„“1; uniform noise- smoothed classiп¬Ғers are state-of-the-art under в„“1 but have poor performance under в„“2). Moreover, some of the recent works [8, 9, 10] show that the existing framework, which uses only the zeroth order information (the function value of the smoothed classiп¬Ғer g(x)), is incapable of producing large certiп¬Ғed radii for в„“p norms with high values of p. Motivated by the two limitations above, we focus our attention on improving the certiп¬Ғed safety region which is agnostic of threat models. To that end, we propose a general framework to provide a larger certiп¬Ғed safety region by better utilizing the information derived from a hard-label classiп¬Ғer. In particular, we summarize our contributions as follows: 1. We propose a general framework that calculates a certiп¬Ғed safety region of a smoothed classiп¬Ғer g, around an input point x, by exploiting the estimated local properties (e.g. gradient, Hessian, etc.) of the classiп¬Ғer gat x. 2. We give a threat-model-agnostic asymptotic-optimality result for smoothed classiп¬Ғers ob- tained by using standard Gaussian as the smoothing measure, i.e. gaussian-smoothed classiп¬Ғers. Using Theorem 2 in Section 3.1, we show that theoretically it is possible to produce arbitrarily tight certiп¬Ғcates for any classiп¬Ғer1. As a consequence, we see that the impossibility results for the existing framework cannot be extended to certiп¬Ғcates obtained using higher-order information. 3. We motivate and prove properties, like convexity (Proposition 1) and non-decreasing depen- dence on angle (Proposition 2), regarding the certiп¬Ғed safety regions of gaussian-smoothed classiп¬Ғers produced using the zeroth and п¬Ғrst-order local information. Using these properties, we give formulas for calculating certiп¬Ғed radii for gaussian-smoothed classiп¬Ғers under в„“p threat models and their subspace variants with p= 1,2,вҲһ. 4. We design new efп¬Ғcient estimators (see Table 1) to provide high-conп¬Ғdence interval esti- mates of relevant п¬Ғrst-order information about the classiп¬Ғer since the naive Monte-Carlo estimators have prohibitively high sample complexity. Finally, we use the 3rd and the 4th contributions above to empirically verify the effectiveness of the new certiп¬Ғcation framework in providing state-of-the-art certiп¬Ғed accuracy for multiple threat models simultaneously. In particular, our proposed framework substantially boosts the certiп¬Ғed accuracy for в„“1 norm and subspace в„“2 norm while maintaining (at times marginally improving) the state-of-the-art near-optimal results for в„“2 norm. On the CIFAR10 dataset, our results for the в„“вҲһnorm also show improvement over the state-of-the-art bounds given by Gaussian smoothing. 2 Background and Related Works 2.1 Related Works Randomized Smoothing. Randomized smoothing was initially introduced as a heuristic defense by [11] and [12]. Later, [3] formulated it as a certiп¬Ғcation method using ideas from differential privacy, which was then improved by [ 6] using Renyi divergence. For gaussian-smoothed classiп¬Ғers, [ 5] made the certiп¬Ғed bounds worst-case-optimal in the context of certiп¬Ғed в„“2 norm radii by using the Neyman-Pearson Lemma, while authors of [13] combined the certiп¬Ғcation method with adversarial training to further improve the empirical results. Along another line of works, some extended existing certiп¬Ғcation methods to get better в„“p norm certiп¬Ғed radii using different smoothing distributions, e.g. 1For this result, the optimality assumes we abstain at points where the top-1 class has probability lower than 0.5. 2a discrete distribution for в„“0 certiп¬Ғcates [14], the Laplace distribution for в„“1 certiп¬Ғcates [15], and the generalized Gaussian distribution for в„“вҲһcertiп¬Ғcates [16]. Recently, [8] proposed a general method for п¬Ғnding the optimal smoothing distribution given any threat model, as well as a framework for calculating the certiп¬Ғed robustness for the smoothed classiп¬Ғer. Impossibility Results. Recently, a number of works have shown that for в„“p norm threat models with large p, it is impossible to give a big certiп¬Ғed radius ( O(d 1 pвҲ’1 2 ) where dis the input dimension) while retaining a high standard accuracy. In particular, the results on в„“вҲһthreat model given in [ 9, 10] and the results on в„“p (for sufп¬Ғciently large p) threat models given in [ 8] establish a certiп¬Ғcation/accuracy trade-off, which also exaggerates the need for an extended and generalized framework that breaks the conп¬Ғned trade-off and impossibility results. 2.2 Preliminaries and Notations Generally, a classiп¬Ғer in machine learning is represented as a function f that maps from the feature space Rd to a probability vector over all the classes Y. We use Fto denote the set of all classiп¬Ғers f : Rd вҶҰвҶ’вҲҶY, where вҲҶYis a probability simplex collecting all possible probability vectors with dimension Y. In this paper, we work under the restrictive black-box model, i.e., we assume we only have access to the class labels outputted by classiп¬Ғer f. For ease of use, we consider the output of f to be the hard-thresholding of the probability vector, i.e. the output is a one-hot encoding vector. Given a black-box classiп¬Ғer f вҲҲF, randomized smoothing is a technique that creates a smoothed classiп¬Ғer gby convolving f (hereafter referred to as the base classiп¬Ғer) with a smoothing measure Вө, i.e., g = f вӢҶВө. We use GВө F to denote the class of these smoothed functions and вӢҶdenotes the convolution operator. Note that the smoothing measure Вөis a probability measure and each smoothed function gвҲҲGВө Fdeп¬Ғnes a mapping from Rd to вҲҶY. Thus, we have GВө FвҠӮF. 3 A General Framework for Randomized Smoothing Given a smoothing measure Вө, the certiп¬Ғed safety region around a point xis given as the subset of the space over which the classiп¬Ғer output is guaranteed not to change. In practice, this involves estimating some local information about garound the point xand giving the certiп¬Ғed safety region as the common subset (intersection) of the safety region of all classiп¬Ғer hвҲҲGВө Fwhose local properties match the estimated local information. In the current literature, the only local information used is the function value g(x) [5, 13]; however, more local information of g(x), such as the gradient or higher-order derivatives, can in fact be exploited for deriving better certiп¬Ғed radii. Under the proposed general framework for calculating certiп¬Ғed radii, it is easy to see that adding more local constraints ( Hx i ) in Equation (2) gives a bigger value of px(z) for any x,z which makes the super-level set of px, equivalently the certiп¬Ғed safety region, bigger. In the following subsection, we study the properties of functions in GВө Fto get some motivation about which information might help us achieve a larger certiп¬Ғed safety region. Then, we provide an example usage of this framework that exploits additional local higher-order information to give larger certiп¬Ғed radii. The proofs of all theoretical results hereafter are supplemented in the appendix. 3.1 Regularity Properties of GВө F For any black-box classiп¬Ғer f, the function map between the input pixel-space Rd and the prediction вҲҶYis discontinuous. As a result, the higher-order derivatives might not always exist for the smoothed function gfor smoothing with general probability measures Вө. Therefore, it is crucial to establish conditions on the probability measure Вөthat guarantee the existence of higher-order derivatives for g at all points in the pixel-space Rd. We give this in the following theorem: Theorem 1. If вҲҖОұ вҲҲNd, вҲ« Rd |DОұ xВө(yвҲ’x)|dy2 is п¬Ғnite, then GВө F вҠӮCвҲһ. Therefore in this section we complete the framework by giving ways to get high conп¬Ғdence estimates of vector y(1). Recalling that the hard-label classiп¬Ғer f is not continuous and hence not differentiable, we need to use the zeroth order information about f to approximate y(1) , i.e., use the result from Corollary 1.1 : y(1) = EyвҲјВө[ y Пғ2 f(x+ y)]. As the estimator y Пғ2 f(x+ y) is noisy [13], approximating the expectation using Monte-Carlo sampling is hard, i.e., the number of samples required to achieve a non-trivial high-conп¬Ғdence estimate of y(1) scales linearly with the input dimension. In practice, this translates to a sample complexity of 10 billion for CIFAR and near a trillion for Imagenet.
