However, we see that estimation of оө№оө№y(1)оө№оө№ 1 still scales linearly with dmaking it impractical. Thus, the в„“вҲһnorm certiп¬Ғed radius is still given as 1вҲҡ d of the в„“2 norm certiп¬Ғed radius. Finally, for estimating the в„“p norm (p = 1,2,вҲһ) over a subspace S we show that we can use the corresponding estimators from Table 1 except withXS n1 = PSXn1, YS n2 = PSYn2 and d= dS where PS is the orthogonal projection matrix for Sand dS is the dimension of S. 5 Experimental Results We empirically study the performance of the new certiп¬Ғcation schemes on standard image classiп¬Ғca- tion datasets, CIFAR10 and Imagenet. We reuse the models given by Cohen et al. [5] and calculate the certiп¬Ғed accuracy at radius Rby counting the samples of the test set that are correctly classiп¬Ғed by the smoothed classiп¬Ғer gwith certiп¬Ғed radii of at least R. For both our proposed certiп¬Ғcate and the baseline certiп¬Ғcate [5], we use a failure probability of Оұ= 0.001 and N = 200,000 samples for CIFAR10 and N = 1,250,000 samples for Imagenet. For в„“вҲһradius we require a lot more samples to get better results as our current estimator is too noisy. In order to show the possibility presented by our approach, we also provide results for the в„“вҲһcertiп¬Ғed radius for CIFAR10 estimated using 4,000,000 samples. In our plots, we present, for each threat model the upper envelopes of certiп¬Ғed accuracies attained over the range of considered ПғвҲҲ{0.12,0.25,0.50,1.00}. Further experiments (including all Imagenet experiments) are given in the appendix. 85.1 Certiп¬Ғed Accuracy As expected we see from Figure 2b that the new framework gives only marginal improvement over the в„“2 radius certiп¬Ғed by existing methods. This follows from the fact that the existing methods already produce near-optimal certiп¬Ғed в„“2 radii. However, certifying a signiп¬Ғcantly bigger certiп¬Ғed safety region allows us to give signiп¬Ғcant improvements over the certiп¬Ғed в„“1 radius, the в„“вҲһradius and the subspace в„“2 radii (the subspace considered here is the red channel of the image, i.e., we only allow perturbations over red component of the RGB pixels of the image). We note here that considering the performance for individual threat models, the в„“1 norm certiп¬Ғed accuracy given here is still smaller than the existing state-of-the-art. However, the current state-of-the-art for в„“1 certiп¬Ғed radius uses the uniform smoothing distribution which performs quite poorly for в„“2 norm radius. If we consider multiple threat models simultaneously the proposed method gives the best joint performance. Similar п¬Ғndings are also reported for Imagenet experiments (given in the appendix). Moreover, we see that for the certiп¬Ғed в„“вҲһradius the new method gives an improvement over the certiп¬Ғed в„“вҲһradius provided by existing methods for Gaussian noise. As Gaussian noise was shown to achieve the best possible certiп¬Ғed в„“вҲһradius for existing methods, this provides an improvement over the best possible в„“вҲһradius certiп¬Ғable using existing methods. Although the improvements are small in magnitude, we believe they can be improved upon by using tighter estimators for оө№оө№y(1)оө№оө№ 1. 6 Conclusion and Future Work In this work, we give a new direction for improving the robustness certiп¬Ғcates for randomized- smoothing-based classiп¬Ғers. We have shown that even in the black-box (hard-label classiп¬Ғer) setting, leveraging more information about the distribution of the labels among the sampled points allows us to certify larger regions and thus guarantee large certiп¬Ғed radii against multiple threat models simultaneously. We have shown this to hold theoretically and also demonstrated it on CIFAR and Imagenet classiп¬Ғers. Additionally, for gaussian smoothing, the proposed framework gives a way to circumvent the recently-proposed impossibility results and also promises a threat-model-agnostic asymptotic-optimality result. However, we note that the п¬Ғrst-order smoothing technique given in this paper is only a proof-of-concept to show it is possible to better leverage local information to certify larger safety regions without changing the smoothing measure. In future, this work could be extended to derive and use, for any given threat model, the best local information to exploit in order to improve the certiп¬Ғcates for that threat model. 9Broader Impact In recent years, machine learning and intelligent systems have started to be widely adopted into everyday life, including several safety-critical applications. The wide-spread use of these systems requires them to be held to a higher level of scrutiny. One such requirement is вҖңrobustnessвҖқ, i.e., the systemsвҖҷ prediction should not be too sensitive to small input perturbations. In general, unde- fended/vanilla deep learning models have been found to be extremely sensitive to small imperceptible perturbations. This in turn has given rise to many defense techniques, able to overcome вҖңexistingвҖқ threats to robustness. However most of these models have later proved to be ineffective against newer threats. In turn, this has then given rise to a class of techniques which provide some mathematical guarantees about the robustness of their predictions. In this paper, we extended one such framework that provides some mathematically provable guarantees against any вҖңsingle individualвҖқ threat model chosen from a large class of threat models. Speciп¬Ғcally, we provided a way to construct models with some mathematically provable guarantees against вҖңmultiple simultaneousвҖқ threat models. The beneп¬Ғts of this contribution include providing a more holistic picture of the robustness guarantees for deployed models. This can hopefully bring us a step closer to trustworthy AI. Since this work helps building models that give some guarantees on their behavior, we hope it would also lead to the wider adoption of deep learning models. Moreover, we hope that the guarantees given by these models would allow people to have some improved sense of security when using deep-learning-based products. Considering intensive applications such as product matching, categorization in assembly lines or video surveillance that require long periods of hard effort, robust models will give more reliable and efп¬Ғcient means of achieving the task and considerably reducing the burden on humans. As for the potentially negative effects of this contribution, we feel that any progress towards robust- ness certiп¬Ғcation could easily become a double-edged sword. This is because, if adopted blindly (i.e. speciп¬Ғcally without extremely careful attention to the types of guarantees provided and most impor- tantly those NOT provided), it may give also the false sense of security that current deep-learning systems are already ready for deployment. These robustness concerns are one of the major bottlenecks for the adoption of deep-learning models in safety-critical applications such as self-driving car and air trafп¬Ғc control systems. hence the ability to provide вҖңsomeвҖқ robustness guarantees might result in wide premature adoption of deep learning models in such applications. We would like to candidly admit to developer and user readers of this paper that the presence of some robustness guarantees for a model does not mean we understand the model or that it is completely safe to deploy it. To the contrary the authors of this paper believe that we are still quite far from such safe adoption scenario. Further issues lie also beyond robustness. A negative effect of this publication and publication like this on robustness, is might give the highly incorrect impression that with robustness one can feel safe in deploying AI systems in society. On the contrary we would also like to candidly admit and remind the readers that deep-learning-based models suffer from problems such as lack of accuracy guarantees for out-of-distribution data , lack of fairness, lack of explain-ability and many others that MUST to be solved before AI systems are viable for real-world applications. More speciп¬Ғcally about the lack of accuracy guarantees for out-of-distribution data: the robustness of a model does not necessarily mean its prediction is always accurate. Robustness and accuracy are indeed two disjoint concepts. While it is well-known that an accurate prediction might not be robust, it is also essential to keep in mind that a robust prediction need not always be accurate. In other words, some models may generate a prediction that is indeed highly robust (i.e. does not change upon perturbation) but consistently incorrect! As a result, in applications such as air trafп¬Ғc control systems, the models might display extremely bad behaviour because of situations not present in its training, while the presence of some robustness guarantees might give a false sense of security to an inexperience user (e.g. not familiar with the admittedly subtle mathematical intricacies of different threat models), that these systems are completely fault-tolerant. In conclusion, we would like to remark that although this paper helps to take a step towards building more trustworthy AI, that goal is indeed still quite far-off. 10References [1] C. Szegedy, W. For any Оұ вүҘ2eвҲ’d 16 , given t = вҲҡ вҲ’k2 вҲҡ 2d n1n2 log Оұ 2 , Пөu = вҲҡ вҲ’k(n1+n2) log Оұ 2 2n1n2(XTn1Yn2+t) , Пөl = вҲҡ вҲ’k(n1+n2) log Оұ 2 2n1n2(XTn1Yn2вҲ’t) , P (оө№оө№оө№y(1) оө№оө№оө№ 2 вүӨ вҲҡ XTn1Yn2 + t вҲҡ 1 + Пө2u вҲ’Пөu ) вүҘ1 вҲ’Оұ, P (оө№оө№оө№y(1) оө№оө№оө№ 2 вүҘ вҲҡ XTn1Yn2 вҲ’t вҲҡ 1 + Пө2 l + Пөl ) вүҘ1 вҲ’Оұ Proof. Using Theorem 4 and the properties of subgaussian random vectors, we see that Xn1 вҲј subG( k n1 ),Yn2 вҲјsubG( k n2 ). Then, using Theorem 5 we get the required values of t,Пөu,Пөl. 26E Additional Experiments Here, we give additional experiments on the Imagenet dataset. We reuse the models given by Cohen et al. [5] and calculate the certiп¬Ғed accuracy at radius Rby counting the samples of the test set that are correctly classiп¬Ғed by the smoothed classiп¬Ғer gwith certiп¬Ғed radii of at least R. For both our proposed certiп¬Ғcate and the baseline certiп¬Ғcate [ 5], we use a failure probability of Оұ= 0.001 and N = 200,000 samples for CIFAR and N = 1,250,000 samples for Imagenet. In the following plots we give a more detailed account of the improvement seen by using both the п¬Ғrst and zeroth order information. For every trained model (depending on variance Пғused during training), we give the certiп¬Ғed accuracy under в„“2 norm threat model, в„“1 norm threat model and the subspace в„“2 norm threat model. Figure 4: Certiп¬Ғed Accuracy for Imagenet seen under various threat models and Пғvalues. The scale of x-axis is different for the 3 different models (denoted by training noise variance) as the certiп¬Ғed radii we get for these three models have different ranges. The п¬Ғndings here are similar to the ones reported for CIFAR. As expected we see from Figure 4 that the smallest improvements are for в„“2 norm threat model where the new framework gives only marginal improvement over the в„“2 radius certiп¬Ғed by existing methods. This follows from the fact that the existing methods already produce near-optimal certiп¬Ғed в„“2 radii. However, certifying a signiп¬Ғcantly bigger certiп¬Ғed safety region allows us to give signiп¬Ғcant improvements over the certiп¬Ғed в„“1 radius and the subspace в„“2 radii (the subspace considered here is the red channel of the image, i.e., we only allow perturbations over red component of the RGB pixels of the image). From these п¬Ғgures we are also able to see that, for any given model, most of the improvement in certiп¬Ғed accuracy occurs at smaller values of radius R. We think one of the causes for this is the interval size of our estimates of y(0),y(1). So, we give the following results using larger number of samples to estimate both y(0),y(1). In Figure 5 we show the results by using the observed values from experiments us- ing Nobs = 1250000 and then constructing the estimates assuming we used N = 200000,800000,1250000,1600000,3200000,6400000 samples respectively. Using these estimates we get a certiп¬Ғed в„“1 radius for both the existing method and for the proposed method. We see that 27Figure 5: Effect of number of samples used on certiп¬Ғed accuracy. using larger number of samples allows us to get improvements at even larger values ofR. However, we note that it is still not possible to get improvements at very high values of R. We think this would require very precise bounds for y(0),y(1) and thus a very high number of samples. 28

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions:
- Introduces a general, higher-order certification framework for randomized smoothing that enlarges the certified safety region without changing the smoothing distribution.
- Reformulates certification as a nested optimization problem over smoothed classifiers using local constraints (function value, gradient, higher-order derivatives) and solves it via a generalized NeymanвҖ“Pearson approach.
- Proves regularity and asymptotic optimality for Gaussian-smoothed classifiers: Gaussian smoothing yields real-analytic g with infinite-radius Taylor series; with full higher-order info the framework recovers the exact binary safety region, bypassing prior impossibility results tied to zeroth-order methods.
- Derives first-order certification with key properties (convexity of safety region; directional robustness monotone in angle to gradient) and closed-form computations for в„“2, в„“1, в„“вҲһ and subspace в„“p radii.
- Designs efficient, high-confidence estimators for gradient-norm statistics needed for certification (||вҲҮg||1, ||вҲҮg||2, ||вҲҮg||вҲһ, and subspace variants), dramatically reducing sample complexity vs naive Monte Carlo.
- Empirically demonstrates significant gains on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet in в„“1 and subspace в„“2 radii, small gains in general в„“2, and modest improvements in в„“вҲһ with larger sample sizes.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- General framework: Given smoothing measure Вө and a base classifier f, define smoothed g=fвӢҶВө. For an input x, define the certified set via px(z)=min_{hвҲҲG_Вө^F} h_c(z) subject to local constraints H_i^x(h)=H_i^x(g) (e.g., h(x), вҲҮh(x), higher derivatives). Safety region is {z | px(z)>0.5} under top-1 vs rest reduction.
- Solution via Generalized NeymanвҖ“Pearson Lemma: For Gaussian Вө=N(0,Пғ^2I), and using first-order constraints y(0)=g(x), y(1)=вҲҮg(x), derive a parametric form for the worst-case h leading to integral equations involving ОҰ(c(x)) that lower bound px(x+Пғrv) (Theorem 3). Basis rotation/scaling isolates components parallel/orthogonal to gradient.
- Theoretical regularity: Show G_Вө^FвҠӮC^вҲһ and, for Gaussian Вө, g is real-analytic with infinite convergence radius; the Taylor series uniquely identifies g (Theorems 1вҖ“2).
- Geometry of first-order certified set: Prove convexity of the certified region and that directional robustness decreases with angle from вҲҮg(x) (Propositions 1вҖ“2). Use these to derive:
  - в„“2 radius via solving ОҰ(w1вҲ’r)вҲ’ОҰ(w2вҲ’r)=0.5 with constraints from y(0), ||вҲҮg||2 (Corollary 3.1).
  - в„“1 radius via worst standard basis direction (projection вҲ’||вҲҮg||вҲһ) and corresponding constraints (Corollary 3.2).
  - в„“вҲһ radius via worst Вұ1/вҲҡd direction (projection вҲ’||вҲҮg||1/вҲҡd) (Corollary 3.3).
  - Subspace в„“p radii (pвҲҲ{1,2,вҲһ}) via projections PSвҲҮg and dual norms (Corollary 3.4).
- Estimation of first-order stats: Show z=w(f(x+w)_cвҲ’1/2) with wвҲјN(0,Пғ^2I) is sub-Gaussian around Пғ^2вҲҮg(x) (Theorem 4). Derive concentration and product-of-sub-Gaussian bounds (Theorem 5) to construct high-confidence estimators for ||вҲҮg||2, ||вҲҮg||вҲһ, and approximate ||вҲҮg||1 (Table 1), including subspace versions by projecting samples.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Datasets: CIFAR-10 and ImageNet.
- Models: Pretrained smoothed classifiers from Cohen et al. (2019); multiple Gaussian noise levels ПғвҲҲ{0.12, 0.25, 0.50, 1.00}.
- Evaluation metric: Certified accuracy at radius R (fraction of test samples both correctly classified by g and certified at вүҘR) under different threat models.
- Threat models: в„“2 (general), в„“1 (general), в„“вҲһ (general), and subspace в„“2 (restricted to single color channelвҖ”red).
- Monte Carlo settings: Failure probability Оұ=0.001; N=200,000 samples per input for CIFAR-10 and N=1,250,000 for ImageNet. For в„“вҲһ on CIFAR-10, also report results with 4,000,000 samples due to higher estimator variance. Report upper envelopes across Пғ.
- Additional analysis: Study the effect of increasing sample size on в„“1 certification (up to 6.4M effective samples) and provide detailed ImageNet plots across Пғ values.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Empirical method uses only first-order information; higher-order certification is not implemented, so asymptotic optimality is theoretical.
- Estimation of ||вҲҮg||1 remains sample-inefficient/noisy; в„“вҲһ improvements are small and often require millions of samples, limiting practicality.
- Gains for general в„“2 radii are marginal because zeroth-order methods are already near-optimal for в„“2.
- Assumes binary reduction (top-1 vs rest) and abstains when top-1 probability вүӨ0.5; optimality statements hold under this relaxation.
- Computational cost is high (200kвҖ“1.25M+ samples per point; more for в„“вҲһ), which may hinder scalability.
- в„“1 certified accuracy does not surpass specialized non-Gaussian smoothing methods tailored for в„“1, though joint performance across norms improves.
- Framework depends on accurate interval estimates of local statistics; uncertainty widens constraints and weakens certificates.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Implement and evaluate higher-order (second-order and beyond) certification to realize the asymptotic benefits and larger multi-norm radii.
- Develop tighter, lower-variance, and more sample-efficient estimators, especially for ||вҲҮg||1, to improve в„“вҲһ certification; explore variance-reduction (antithetic sampling, control variates, importance sampling).
- Jointly train base classifiers to maximize first-/higher-order certifiable regions (e.g., training objectives that shape вҲҮg statistics) while maintaining accuracy.
- Extend the framework to other smoothing measures and hybrid/data-dependent smoothing while preserving agnosticism to threat models.
- Broaden subspace certifications (e.g., semantic/structured subspaces) and multi-threat joint certification objectives.
- Move beyond binary reduction to multi-class-aware certification if feasible.
- Optimize computational pipelines and parallelization to reduce sample costs; theoretical bounds linking sample size to certificate tightness.

research_study_list.title: Accelerating Certified Robustness Training via Knowledge Transfer
research_study_list.abstract:
Training deep neural network classifiers that are certifiably robust against
adversarial attacks is critical to ensuring the security and reliability of
AI-controlled systems. Although numerous state-of-the-art certified training
methods have been developed, they are computationally expensive and scale
poorly with respect to both dataset and network complexity. Widespread usage of
certified training is further hindered by the fact that periodic retraining is
necessary to incorporate new data and network improvements. In this paper, we
propose Certified Robustness Transfer (CRT), a general-purpose framework for
reducing the computational overhead of any certifiably robust training method
through knowledge transfer. Given a robust teacher, our framework uses a novel
training loss to transfer the teacher's robustness to the student. We provide
theoretical and empirical validation of CRT. Our experiments on CIFAR-10 show
that CRT speeds up certified robustness training by $8 \times$ on average
across three different architecture generations while achieving comparable
robustness to state-of-the-art methods. We also show that CRT can scale to
large-scale datasets like ImageNet.
research_study_list.full_text: Accelerating Certiп¬Ғed Robustness Training via Knowledge Transfer Pratik Vaishnavi Stony Brook University pvaishnavi@cs.stonybrook.edu Kevin Eykholt IBM Research kheykholt@ibm.com Amir Rahmati Stony Brook University amir@cs.stonybrook.edu Abstract Training deep neural network classiп¬Ғers that are certiп¬Ғably robust against adver- sarial attacks is critical to ensuring the security and reliability of AI-controlled systems. Although numerous state-of-the-art certiп¬Ғed training methods have been developed, they are computationally expensive and scale poorly with respect to both dataset and network complexity. Widespread usage of certiп¬Ғed training is fur- ther hindered by the fact that periodic retraining is necessary to incorporate new data and network improvements. In this paper, we propose Certiп¬Ғed Robustness Transfer (CRT), a general-purpose framework for reducing the computational over- head of any certiп¬Ғably robust training method through knowledge transfer. Given a robust teacher, our framework uses a novel training loss to transfer the teacherвҖҷs robustness to the student. We provide theoretical and empirical validation of CRT. Our experiments on CIFAR-10 show that CRT speeds up certiп¬Ғed robustness train- ing by 8Г—on average across three different architecture generations while achiev- ing comparable robustness to state-of-the-art methods. We also show that CRT can scale to large-scale datasets like ImageNet. 1 Introduction Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) are susceptible to adversarial evasion attacks [31, 9], that add a small amount of carefully crafted imperceptible noise to an input to reliably trigger misclassiп¬Ғcation. As a defense, numerous training methods have been proposed [25, 40, 35] to grant a classiп¬Ғer empirical robustness. But in the absence of any provable guarantees for this robustness, these defenses were frequently broken [1, 32]. These failures have motivated the development of training methods that grant certiп¬Ғable/provable robustness to a classiп¬Ғer, hence safeguarding them against all attacks (known or unknown) within a pre-determined threat model. Such methods are broadly categorized as either deterministic or probabilistic [23]. Deterministic robustness training methods [12, 26, 33, 34, 28, 10, 41, 30] rely on computing provable bounds on the output neurons of a classiп¬Ғer for a given perturbation budget in the input space. However, the deterministic robustness guarantees provided by these methods come at a high computational cost. Probabilistic robustness training methods address this limitation by providing highly probable (e.g., with 0.99 probability) robustness guarantees at a greatly reduced computational cost. Within this category, randomized smoothing- based methods [19, 3, 29, 22, 20, 7, 37, 39, 16, 15] are considered the state-of-the-art for certiп¬Ғable robustness in the в„“2-space. Even so, these training methods remain an order of magnitude slower than standard training. In commercial applications where constant model re-deployment occurs to provide improvements (see Figure 1), re-training using computationally expensive methods is burdensome. 36th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2022). arXiv:2210.14283v1  [cs.LG]  25 Oct 2022ResNet101 (2016) ResNeXt101 (2017) DLA-X (2018) RegNetX-16GF (2020) ViT-B/16 (2021) 76 80 84 88Top-1 Accuracy (%)  45 60 75 90 Number of Params (M) Figure 1: Evolution of DNN architectures on the ImageNet dataset. We plot the performance (top-1 accuracy) and the number of parameters of a few popular architectures (year of release is noted in brackets). Newer generations attempt to improve performance and/or reduce network parameters. In this work, we reduce the training overhead of randomized smoothing-based robustness training methods with minimal impact on the robustness achieved. We propose Certiп¬Ғed Robustness Transfer (CRT), a knowledge transfer framework that signiп¬Ғcantly speeds up the process of training в„“2 certiп¬Ғably robust image classiп¬Ғers. Given a pre-trained classiп¬Ғer that is certiп¬Ғably robust ( i.e., teacher), CRT trains a new classiп¬Ғer ( i.e., student) that has comparable levels of robustness in a fraction of the time required by state-of-the-art methods. CRT brings down the cost of training certiп¬Ғably robust image classiп¬Ғers to be comparable to standard training while preserving state- of-the-art robustness. On CIFAR-10, CRT speeds up training by an average of 8Г—across three different architecture generations compared to a state-of-the-art robustness training method [ 15]. Furthermore, we show that state-of-the-art robustness training is only necessary to train the initial classiп¬Ғer. Afterward, CRT can be continuously reused to transfer robustness in order to expedite future model re-deployments and greatly reduce costs associated with computational resources. Our contributions can be summarized as follows: вҖў We present Certiп¬Ғed Robustness Transfer (CRT), the п¬Ғrst framework, to our knowledge, that can transfer the robustness of a certiп¬Ғably robust teacher classiп¬Ғer to a new student classiп¬Ғer. CRT greatly reduces the time required to train certiп¬Ғably robust image classiп¬Ғers relative to existing state-of-the-art methods while achieving comparable or better robustness. вҖў We provide a theoretical understanding of CRT, showing how our approach of matching outputs enables robustness transfer between the student and teacher irrespective of the certiп¬Ғed robustness training method used to train the teacher. вҖў On CIFAR-10, we show that CRT trains certiп¬Ғably robust classiп¬Ғers on average8Г—faster than a state-of-the-art method while having comparable or better Average Certiп¬Ғed Radius (by 8% in the best case). Furthermore, CRT reduces the cumulative computational cost of training three classiп¬Ғers by 87.84%. вҖў We also show that CRT can be reused in a recursive manner, thus supporting a continuous re- deployment scenario (e.g., in commercial applications). Finally, we show that CRT remains effective on a large-scale dataset, ImageNet. 2 Background In this section, we brieп¬Ӯy introduce certiп¬Ғed robustness and discuss notable existing methods for training certiп¬Ғably robust image classiп¬Ғers using randomized smoothing. 2.1 Preliminaries Problem Setup. Consider a neural network classiп¬Ғer f parameterized by Оё (denoted fОё) trained to map a given input x вҲҲ Rd to a set of discrete labels Y using a set of i.i.d. samples S= {(x1,y1),(x1,y1),В·В·В· ,(xn,yn)}drawn from a data distribution D. The output of the classiп¬Ғer can be written as fОё(x) = arg maxcвҲҲY zc Оё(x). Here zОё(x) is the softmax output of the classiп¬Ғer and zc Оё(x) denotes the probability that image xbelongs to class c. 2Certiп¬Ғed Robustness via Randomized Smoothing. The robustness of the classiп¬Ғer fОё for a given input pair (x,y) is deп¬Ғned using the radius of the largest в„“2 ball centered at xwithin which fОё has a constant output y. This radius is referred to asrobust radiusand it can mathematically be expressed as: R(fОё; x,y) = { inf fОё(xвҖІ)Мё=fОё(x) вҲҘxвҖІвҲ’xвҲҘ2 , when fОё(x) = y 0 , when fОё(x) Мё= y (1) Within this в„“2-neighborhood of x, fОё is considered to be certiп¬Ғably robust. Therefore, to improve the robustness of a classiп¬Ғer, one needs to maximize this robust radius corresponding to any point sampled from the given data distribution. Directly maximizing the robust radius of a DNN classiп¬Ғer is an NP-hard problem [ 17]. Therefore, several prior works attempt to derive a lower bound for the robust radius [21, 19, 3]. This lower bound, often termed as the certiп¬Ғed radius, satisп¬Ғes the following condition: 0 вүӨCR(fОё; x,y) вүӨR(fОё; x,y), for any fОё, (x, y). In this paper, we utilize the certiп¬Ғed robustness framework derived by Cohen et al. [3] using randomized smoothing. Given a classiп¬Ғer fОё, they п¬Ғrst deп¬Ғne the smooth classiп¬Ғer gОё as: Deп¬Ғnition 2.1. For a given (base) classiп¬Ғer fОё and Пғ >0, the smooth classiп¬Ғer gОё corresponding to fОё is deп¬Ғned as follows: gОё(x) = arg max cвҲҲY PО·вҲјN(0,Пғ2I)(fОё(x+ О·) = c) (2) Simply put, gОё returns the class c, which has the highest probability mass under the Gaussian distribution N(x,Пғ2I). Using Theorem 2.2, they proved that if the smooth classiп¬Ғer correctly classiп¬Ғes a given input x, it is certiп¬Ғably robust at x. They also provided an analytical form of the в„“2 certiп¬Ғed radius at x. Theorem 2.2. Let fОё : Rd вҶҰвҶ’Y be a classiп¬Ғer and gОё be its smoothed version (as deп¬Ғned in Deп¬Ғnition 2.1). For a given input x вҲҲRd and corresponding ground truth y вҲҲY, if gОё correctly classiп¬Ғes xas y, i.e., PО·(fОё(x+ О·) = y) вүҘmax yвҖІМё=y PО·(fОё(x+ О·) = yвҖІ) (3) then gОё is provably robust at xwithin the certiп¬Ғed radius Rgiven by: CR(gОё; x,y) = Пғ 2 [ОҰвҲ’1(PО·(fОё(x+ О·) = y)) вҲ’ОҰвҲ’1(max yвҖІМё=y PО·(fОё(x+ О·) = yвҖІ))] (4) where ОҰ is the c.d.f. of the standard Gaussian distribution. This certiп¬Ғed radius is a tight lower bound of the robust radius deп¬Ғned in Equation 1, i.e., it is impossible to certify gОё at xfor a radius larger than CR. 2.2 Training Methods for Maximizing Certiп¬Ғed Radius In addition to the theoretical framework discussed above, Cohen et al. [3] also propose a simple yet effective method for training the base classiп¬Ғer in a way that maximizes the в„“2 certiп¬Ғed radius of the smooth classiп¬Ғer, as expressed in Equation 4. We include an evaluation of their method in Appendix B.6. Following their work, several other works build upon the randomized smoothing framework and propose training methods that better maximize the в„“2 certiп¬Ғed radius of the smooth classiп¬Ғer. Salman et al. [29] proposed combining adversarial training [ 25] with randomized smooth- ing (called SmoothAdv). They adapted the vanilla PGD attack to target the smooth classiп¬Ғer gОё instead of the base classiп¬Ғer fОё. Zhai et al. [39] proposed a new robustness loss, a hinge loss that en- forces maximization of the soft approximation of the certiп¬Ғed radius. Their method (called MACER) is faster than SmoothAdv as it does not use adversarial training. More recently, Jeong et al. [15] proposed training with a convex combination of samples along the direction of adversarial pertur- bation for each input to regularize over-conп¬Ғdent predictions. Their method (called SmoothMix) is the current state-of-the-art in the domain of в„“2 certiп¬Ғed robust image classiп¬Ғers. Finally, we note the Consistency regularization method proposed by Jeong et al. [16], which adds a regularization loss to existing methods that helps better maximize the certiп¬Ғed radius. 3Table 1: Training on CIFAR-10 using a ResNet110 classiп¬Ғer on a single Nvidia V100 GPU. State-of- the-art robustness training methods signiп¬Ғcantly slow down training compared to standard training. METHOD TRAINING SLOWDOWN FACTOR SMOOTH ADV 46.20Г— MACER 20.86Г— SMOOTH MIX 4.97Г— 3 Maximizing Certiп¬Ғed Radius via Knowledge Transfer Although prior works have proposed methods for increasing the certiп¬Ғed radius of the smooth classiп¬Ғer, their training overhead is signiп¬Ғcant, making them much slower than standard training. As we show in Table 1, training a certiп¬Ғably robust ResNet110 classiп¬Ғer to convergence using SmoothAdv, MACER, and SmoothMix is46.20Г—, 20.86Г—, and 4.97Г—slower, respectively, compared to training a non-robust classiп¬Ғer with standard training. Given constant innovations in architecture design (Figure 1) and the inп¬Ӯux of new data, which may result in various tweaks to deployed networks that elicit retraining, the large overhead of state-of-the- art robustness training methods makes preserving certiп¬Ғed robustness across model re-deployment difп¬Ғcult. Therefore, we propose Certiп¬Ғed Robustness Transfer (CRT), a training method that improves the usability of certiп¬Ғed robustness training methods by dramatically reducing their training overhead while preserving the certiп¬Ғed robustness. Given the base classiп¬Ғer of a pre-trained certiп¬Ғably robust smooth classiп¬Ғer, we leverage the knowledge transfer framework to guide the training of a new base classiп¬Ғer (and its associated robust smooth classiп¬Ғer).1 In this section, we describe our method and provide theoretical justiп¬Ғcation for its effectiveness. 3.1 Transferring Certiп¬Ғed Robustness From Equation 4, it follows that training the base classiп¬Ғer to maximize PО·(fОё(x+ О·) = y) for any given input xwill result in the maximization of the certiп¬Ғed radius associated with the smooth classiп¬Ғer, provided Equation 3 is satisп¬Ғed. Thus, for the base classiп¬Ғer fОё(x), our goal is to maximize the following quantity over the training set: nвҲ‘ i=1 EО·1[fОё(xi + О·) = yi] вүҲ nвҲ‘ i=1 EО·[zyi Оё (xi + О·)] (5) In the above equation, like prior works [3, 29, 39], we leverage the fact that the softmax output of a classiп¬Ғer can be treated as a continuous and differentiable approximation of its arg max output. Methods like SmoothAdv [29], MACER [39] and SmoothMix [15] that target в„“2 certiп¬Ғable robustness propose training objectives that maximize this term. Now, suppose we have a pre-trained base classiп¬Ғer fПҶ. It follows that EО·[zy ПҶ(x+ О·)] вүҘ0. Through straightforward algebraic manipulations (see Appendix A), we derive the following lower bound: nвҲ‘ i=1 EО·[zyi Оё (xi + О·)] вүҘвҲ’ nвҲ‘ i=1 EО·[zyi ПҶ (xi + О·) вҲ’zyi Оё (xi + О·)] (6) That is to say that, for a given input xi, if we minimize the difference between the softmax outputs of the teacher and the student (fПҶ and fОё) corresponding to the correct label yi, we will maximize Equation 5 for the student. However, to ensure that the student has a non-trivial certiп¬Ғed radius, we must also ensure that Equation 3 is satisп¬Ғed. If we assume that Equation 3 holds true for the teacher (i.e., the base classiп¬Ғer of a certiп¬Ғably robust smooth classiп¬Ғer), this condition can also be achieved for the student by matching the overall softmax output of the student to that of the teacher. 3.2 Certiп¬Ғed Robustness Transfer (CRT) Based on the previous discussion, we now describe our method for training a certiп¬Ғably robust classiп¬Ғer through knowledge transfer. First, we obtain a pre-trained base classiп¬Ғer fПҶ, which has been trained using a randomized smoothing based robustness training method as this maximizes 1If no pre-trained classiп¬Ғer is available, we п¬Ғrst train an architecture of lower complexity (i.e., fast to train) compared to the target architecture (Section 5.1). 4Algorithm 1 Certiп¬Ғed Robustness Transfer (CRT) 1: Input: Training data distribution D, certiп¬Ғably robust teacher base classiп¬Ғer fПҶ, noise level Пғ, total training iterations T, learning rate Оұ 2: Output: Certiп¬Ғably robust student base classiп¬Ғer fОё 3: ОёвҶҗrandom initialization 4: iвҶҗ0 5: while i< T do 6: From D, sample a batch of inputs {x1,x2,В·В·В· ,xn}. 7: From N(0,Пғ2I), generate a batch of Gaussian noise samples {О·1,О·2,В·В·В· ,О·n}. 8: li вҶҗ1 n вҲ‘n j=1 вҲҘzПҶ(xj + О·j) вҲ’zОё(xj + О·j)вҲҘ2 9: ОёвҶҗОёвҲ’ОұВ·вҲҮОёli 10: iвҶҗi+ 1 11: end while EО·[zy ПҶ(x+ О·)]. Next, we use fПҶ as a teacher to train a new student base classiп¬Ғer fОё. The student is trained to match the output of the teacher. In doing so, we can maximize the certiп¬Ғed radius of the associated smooth classiп¬Ғer gОё (Equation 6), as well as ensure that Equation 3 is satisп¬Ғed. We describe our implementation in Algorithm 1. Given a batch of inputs, we п¬Ғrst perturb them with additive Gaussian noise. Next, we compute the в„“2 distance between the student and the teacherвҖҷs outputs for these Gaussian perturbed inputs. This distance serves as our loss function, and we update the parameters of the student to minimize this loss. At test time, the classiп¬Ғer fОё is converted to its smooth version gОё following Deп¬Ғnition 2.1. 3.3 Prior Works on Robustness Transfer Several prior works have examined transferring adversarial robustness between classiп¬Ғers, but these works have been limited to transferring empirical rather than certiп¬Ғed robustness [2, 8, 14, 42, 43]. Of note is the work by Goldblum et al. [8] in which they combine adversarial training [ 25] with knowledge distillation [13]. They show that distilling knowledge from a large network to a small network improves its empirical robustness as compared to training the small network on its own, but their method makes no effort to improve the computational cost of adversarial training. 4 Evaluation Our goal is to improve the usability of randomized smoothing based robustness training methods. In this section, we demonstrate how CRT enables the reuse of an existing certiп¬Ғably robust classiп¬Ғer to train new certiп¬Ғably robust classiп¬Ғers at signiп¬Ғcantly reduced training cost compared to prior methods. In our п¬Ғrst experiment, we train a ResNet110 classiп¬Ғer with a state-of-the-art method, i.e., SmoothMix [15]), and use CRT to transfer its robustness to train several newer generation classiп¬Ғers. In a second experiment, we recursively use CRT to train a newer generation classiп¬Ғer using the previous generation classiп¬Ғer that was also trained using CRT. In each experiment, we compare the certiп¬Ғed robustness of classiп¬Ғer trained using CRT against a classiп¬Ғer trained using SmoothMix (Section 4.1). We п¬Ғnd that classiп¬Ғers trained using CRT are similarly robust as when trained using SmoothMix but only require a fraction of training time (Section 4.2). Our main results are generated using the CIFAR-10 dataset [18], but we also demonstrate the effectiveness of CRT on ImageNet [5] (Section 5.3). Both these datasets are open-source and free for non-commercial use. Architectures. We use several popular DNN architectures that were proposed to either improve upon the visual recognition performance of the previous generation architectures or preserve performance while requiring signiп¬Ғcantly fewer parameters (or both). For the CIFAR-10 experiments, we use ResNet110 [11], ResNeXt29-2x64d [36], DLA [38], and RegNetX_200MF [27].2 Training details. All SmoothMix classiп¬Ғers were trained using the code made available by the authors3 and the hyperparameters reported by them [ 15]. All CRT classiп¬Ғers were trained using Stochastic Gradient Descent till convergence (200 epochs), with a batch size of 128. Further hyperparameter details are available in Appendix C.1. Unless speciп¬Ғed, we report results for noise 2We use the CIFAR-10 version of these architectures, code [MIT License]: https://github.com/ kuangliu/pytorch-cifar. 3SmoothMix code [MIT License]: https://github.com/jh-jeong/smoothmix 5level Пғ = 0.25 in the main paper. Additional results for higher noise levels Пғ = 0.5 and 1.0 are reported in Appendix B.1. Evaluation Metrics. We report our results using two metrics. First, as done in prior work, we measure the certiп¬Ғed robustness of a classiп¬Ғer based on (1) the certiп¬Ғed test accuracy at в„“2 radius r4, which is deп¬Ғned as the fraction of test set inputs that the smooth classiп¬Ғer classiп¬Ғes correctly within an в„“2 ball of radius rcentered at each input, and (2) average certiп¬Ғed radius (ACR), which is the average of the certiп¬Ғed radius across all inputs in the test set: ACR(gОё) = 1 ntest ntestвҲ‘ i=1 CR(gОё; xi,yi) On CIFAR-10, we compute these metrics using the entire test set. Second, we measure training time of a classiп¬Ғer based on the per-epoch time and total training time. The total training time is computed once the modelвҖҷs loss has converged. All classiп¬Ғers were trained on the same machine with a single Nvidia Titan V GPU. 4.1 Certiп¬Ғed Robustness Comparison Standard CRT Training. Given a ResNet110 classiп¬Ғer trained using SmoothMix, we transfer its robustness to several newer generation classiп¬Ғers. We compare the certiп¬Ғed robustness of these classiп¬Ғers with their SmoothMix trained versions. The results are summarized in Table 2. We observe that using CRT does not reduce the certiп¬Ғed robustness of the trained classiп¬Ғer compared to training with SmoothMix. In fact, interestingly, CRT trained classiп¬Ғers exhibit higher certiп¬Ғed robustness compared to their SmoothMix baseline. Not only do CRT trained classiп¬Ғers have higher ACR (improvement of 8.1% in the best case), they also exhibit higher certiп¬Ғed accuracy at differentв„“2 radii. Furthermore, CRT remains effective even as the generation gap between the student and the teacher increases. This implies that the same teacher can potentially be reused indeп¬Ғnitely, amortizing the teacherвҖҷs training cost to a constant. These results empirically validate our theoretical justiп¬Ғcation of CRT. Finally, we note that in Table 2, the accuracy on clean inputs and ACR of CRT trained classiп¬Ғers follow the same trend as in Figure 1, thus motivating the need for periodic model re-deployment to incorporate architectural improvements. The empirical results are to be expected given our theoretical understanding of CRT: In order to train a robust student, we only require that the teacher is already robust ( i.e., satisп¬Ғes the condition of Theorem 2.2) irrespective of the training method used to achieve robustness. In Section 5.2, we will highlight the relationship between the teacherвҖҷs training method and the robustness of a CRT trained student. 4.2 Training Time Comparison Having established that CRT effectively transfers certiп¬Ғed robustness between classiп¬Ғers, we now evaluate its training overhead. For comparison, we also evaluate the training overhead of SmoothMix. In Table 4, we report the per-epoch time and total time of training different architectures with each method. For brevity, we only compare the training time for standard CRT.5 We observe that the per- epoch time of CRT is signiп¬Ғcantly lower than SmoothMix. Similarly, when trained until convergence, the total training time of CRT is signiп¬Ғcantly lower. Across the three architectures that we run our experiments on, CRT achieves an average epoch time speedup of 10.75Г—. Comparing overall training times, CRT speeds up training by, on average, 8.06Г—. If we consider the real-world scenario where the model has to be periodically redeployed to incorporate architectural improvements, the cumulative training time using SmoothMix is 96.21 hours as each new architecture is trained from scratch. With CRT, the cumulative time is reduced to 11.70 hours representing a 87.84% savings in costs associated with computational resources. TeacherвҖҷs availability.So far, we assumed the availability of a certiп¬Ғably robust teacher (ResNet110). We argue that this is a reasonable assumption as the amortized cost associated with the one-time training of a robust teacher is negligible across many generations of the model. Regardless, in Section 5.1, we examine a scenario where the teacher is unavailable. Under this scenario, we demonstrate how CRT can be used to speedup the training of ResNet110 for use as teacher. The section layout is as follows: in Section 5.1, we discuss the scenario in which a certiп¬Ғably robust teacher is not readily available and demonstrate how CRT can still speed up robustness training; in Section 5.2, we examine how the method used to train the teacher affects the robustness of the student; in Section 5.3, we study the scalability of CRT using the ImageNet dataset; in Section 5.4, we compare CRT with a closely related prior work on fast certiп¬Ғed robustness training, i.e., Consistency regularization [16]; in Section 5.5, we discuss the limitations of CRT; in Section 5.6, we address the broader impact of CRT. 5.1 Teacher Not Available WeвҖҷve designed CRT under the assumption that a certiп¬Ғably robust teacher is already available. However, even if a certiп¬Ғably robust teacher is not available, CRT can still speed up training. Given a certiп¬Ғable robust training method and a large network architecture, we can reduce the training overhead by robustly training a comparatively smaller network п¬Ғrst. Then, we can use CRT to transfer the robustness of the small network to a larger network. In Table 5, we present results for such a process. First, we trained a ResNet20 network using SmoothMix, then we used CRT to train a ResNet110 network. We compare the robustness of a ResNet110 trained using this process with one trained using SmoothMix. As we can see, the CRT ResNet110 network has comparable robustness with the SmoothMix ResNet110 network. However, even when adding the teacher and student training times, CRT still speeds up training by approximately 2Г—relative to SmoothMix. Overall, we observe a slight variation in the robustness of the CRT trained networks depending on the teachers training method. Based on Equation 6, this is expected as maximizing the teacherвҖҷs performance will in turn maximize the performance of the student. Our empirical results align with this reasoning: the MACER teacher was the least robust of the three methods, and its student is similarly the least robust of the students. However, in all cases, the CRT trained network obtained certiп¬Ғed robustness comparable to its teacher. 5.3 Scalability Here, we study the effectiveness of CRT on a large-scale dataset, i.e., ImageNet. In all cases, we observe that students achieve certiп¬Ғed robustness comparable to their respective teachers. However, another closely related work was recently published by Jeong & Shin [ 16], which shows state-of-the-art ACR and potential training time improvements. They proposed a consistency regularization loss that improves the certiп¬Ғed robustness of smooth classiп¬Ғers by enforcing the base classiп¬ҒerвҖҷs soft outputs to be consistent across multiple noisy copies of a given input. Therefore, their additional computational overhead scales linearly with the number of noisy samples used to compute the consistency loss. With respect to computational overhead, CRT adds only one forward pass,i.e., the pass through the teacher. When paired with Gaussian data augmentation training, their regularization loss signiп¬Ғcantly improves the certiп¬Ғed robustness of a smooth classiп¬Ғer. By applying their regularization loss over only two noisy copies of the input, they can achieve better certiп¬Ғed robustness than prior state-of-the- art robustness training methods like MACER [39] and SmoothAdv [29]. The key difference between CRT and consistency regularization is in the training overhead when combined with other state-of-the-art certiп¬Ғed training methods. Consistency regularization augments classiп¬Ғer training with an additional loss term. Therefore, their training overhead is dominated by the training method selected. In their experiments, they focused on Gaussian data augmentation, which adds little to no training overhead relative to standard training. However, if a more computationally intensive method was selected (e.g., MACER), they remark their training overhead would dramatically increase. With respect to CRT, if a teacher is available ( i.e., a previous generation model), the overhead of CRT is agnostic to the training method. If it is not available, we demonstrated in Section 5.1, that CRT can still greatly reduce training overhead. For interested readers, we include results for transferring robustness from a teacher trained using Consistency regularization in Appendix B.4. 95.5 Limitations In this paper, we use probabilistic certiп¬Ғed robustness methods as they rely on Theorem 2.2 and, thus, are designed to maximize the certiп¬Ғed radius (Equation 4). We found that deterministic methods (e.g., CROWN-IBP [41]) impose a stricter training requirement on the base teacher classiп¬Ғer. For a given input, deterministic training methods require the base classiп¬Ғer to be correct for all inputs within the в„“2-norm ball, rather than simply be likely to correctly classify inputs within the в„“2-norm ball. This restriction lowers the potential ACR of the smooth teacher classiп¬Ғer, which also lowers the ACR of the student trained using CRT. For example, when using CROWN-IBP [41] to train a ResNeXt base classiп¬Ғer, the ACR for the corresponding smooth classiп¬Ғer is only 0.064. When transferring the robustness of this ResNeXt classiп¬Ғer to a WideResNet34-10 student, we get an ACR of 0.065. Additionally, we note that the classiп¬Ғer architectures we present in the paper are restricted to CNNs. Recently, a new class of transformer-based image classiп¬Ғers [6, 24, 4] have been proposed that show improved performance over CNN classiп¬Ғers. We brieп¬Ӯy studied the effectiveness of CRT when transferring robustness between CNN and transformer architectures using ViT [6] and present the results in Appendix B.2, but further exploration is needed. Finally, CRT has only been studied using the в„“2 norm and image data due to the limitations of current certiп¬Ғed robustness training methods. 5.6 Broader Impacts As we have shown, our work improves the efп¬Ғciency of training certiп¬Ғably robust classiп¬Ғers, in an effort to improve the security of AI-powered systems. Beyond the broad negative societal impacts of machine learning, we are not aware of any impacts speciп¬Ғc to our work. 6 Conclusion In this paper, we proposed the п¬Ғrst general-purpose framework to speed up the training of certiп¬Ғably robust classiп¬Ғers using knowledge transfer and randomized smoothing. Our proposed method, Certiп¬Ғed Robustness Transfer (CRT) enables transferring the certiп¬Ғed robustness of a classiп¬Ғer to another classiп¬Ғer at a cost comparable to standard training. We provided a theoretical understanding of CRT and provided empirical evidence of its effectiveness. On CIFAR-10, we showed that across several generations of classiп¬Ғer architectures, CRT trained classiп¬Ғers 8Г—faster than when using a state-of-the-art training method, while achieving comparable or better certiп¬Ғed robustness. Furthermore, CRT can reduce the training overhead of certiп¬Ғed robustness training methods even when an initial robust classiп¬Ғer is not present. The use of machine learning in security and safety critical environments motivates a need for models with certiп¬Ғably robust performance, but the training overhead of existing certiп¬Ғed robustness training methods inhibits their usability. Our work addresses this issue, especially for commercial applications where periodical model re-deployment is inevitable. [N/A] 13A Justiп¬Ғcation for CRT Training Objective Following from the discussion in Section 3.1, we want to maximize EО·[zy Оё(x+ О·)]. Given a non- negative random variable t= EО·[zy ПҶ(x+ О·)], we have EО·[zy Оё(x+ О·)] вүҘEО·[zy Оё(x+ О·)] вҲ’t Here zПҶ is the soft output associated with classiп¬Ғer fПҶ that has been trained independently of fОё. Therefore, we have EО·[zy Оё(x+ О·)] вүҘEО·[zy Оё(x+ О·)] вҲ’EО·[zy ПҶ(x+ О·)] вүҘEО·[zy Оё(x+ О·) вҲ’zy ПҶ(x+ О·)] вүҘвҲ’EО·[zy ПҶ(x+ О·) вҲ’zy Оё(x+ О·)] This implies that maximizing EО·[zy Оё(x+ О·)] is equivalent to minimizing EО·[zy ПҶ(x+ О·) вҲ’zy Оё(x+ О·)]. B Additional Results B.1 Higher Noise Level In the main paper, we conduct experiments on CIFAR-10 using noise levelПғ= 0.25 only. Here, we report our main set of results on CIFAR-10 (Table 3) using higher Пғvalues. In Table 8, we report results using Пғ= 0.5 and in Table 9, we report results using Пғ= 1.0. B.2 Using ViT [6] In the main paper, we used Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based architectures. However, there is another recently developed class of architectures that use Transformers for the task of image classiп¬Ғcation [ 6, 24, 4]. In Table 10, we present results measuring the effectiveness of CRT in transferring robustness from ResNet110 (a CNN-based classiп¬Ғer) to ViT [6] (a Transformer-based classiп¬Ғer). For comparison, we also report results obtained on training ViT with SmoothMix. CRT trained ViT classiп¬Ғers perform comparable or better than their SmoothMix counterparts. B.3 Training SmoothMix Classiп¬Ғers with CRT Hyperparameters For generating results using prior methods, we strictly adhere to the hyperparameters reported by them. However, the hyperparameters that we use for CRT training is different than the ones used by prior methods (see Table 16). In Table 11, we report results obtained on using CRT training hyperparameters with SmoothMix. We note that there is not a signiп¬Ғcant difference in the robustness achieved using the two sets of hyperparameters. B.4 Training Teacher with Consistency Regularization [16] For our main set of results on CIFAR-10, we focused on SmoothMix. In Section 5.4, we discussed Consistency Regularization recently proposed by Jeong & Shin [ 16] as another method to attain certiп¬Ғably robust classiп¬Ғers with at a cost comparable to standard training depending on the setting. Here, we show results when Consistency Regularization is used to train the teacher classiп¬Ғer in Tables 12 and 13. For comparison, in Table 12, we also report the results for training the classiп¬Ғers using Consistency Regularization. As with other training methods, CRT is effective in transferring the robustness of the teacher classiп¬Ғer irrespective of the training method. B.5 ImageNet Results In Table 14, we present an additional ImageNet result using a different student-teacher pair. We note that, as in Table 7, CRT remains effective. B.6 Gaussian Data Augmentation Baseline Along with the theoretical framework for creating certiп¬Ғably robust image classiп¬Ғers using random- ized smoothing, Cohen et al. [3] also proposed a simple yet effective method for training classiп¬Ғers with high certiп¬Ғed robustness within this framework. This method involves training the base classi- п¬Ғer with Gaussian data augmentation. To date, this method remains the fastest way to train classi- п¬Ғers with non-trivial certiп¬Ғed robustness using the randomized smoothing framework. However, this method is not as sophisticated as the more recently proposed methods, and so yields much poorer cer- tiп¬Ғed robustness than them.
