We now show the last property of the valid sparsity pattern. This is the only part where we use that the network is dense. For any network architecture the п¬Ғrst two conditions hold. We will use the fact that the maximal cliques of a chordal graph form a valid sparsity pattern (see for example Lasserre (2006)). Because the network is dense, we see that the clique Ii is composed of the neuron in the last layer s(dвҲ’1,i) and all neurons in the previous layers. We show that this graph is chordal. Let (a1,...,a r,a1) be a cycle of length at least 4 ( r вүҘ4). notice that because neurons in the last layer are not connected between them in ЛҶG, no two consecutive neurons in this cycle belong to the last layer. This implies that in the subsequence(a1,a2,a3,a4,a5) at most three belong to the last layer. A simple analysis of all cases implies that it contains at least two nonconsecutive neurons not in the last layer. Neurons not in the last layer are always connected in ЛҶG. This constitutes a chord. This shows that ЛҶGd is a chordal graph. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces LiPopt, a polynomial-optimization framework that computes valid, progressively tighter upper bounds on the Lipschitz constant of neural networks, with a focus on the в„“вҲһ norm. Key ideas: (i) cast Lipschitz estimation as a polynomial optimization problem via a norm-gradient polynomial; (ii) solve via a hierarchy of linear programs using KrivineвҖҷs positivity certificate; (iii) exploit network sparsity through a sparse Krivine certificate derived from the computational graph to dramatically reduce LP size; (iv) provide an alternative QCQP reformulation with ShorвҖҷs SDP relaxation; (v) demonstrate tighter bounds and better compute/accuracy trade-offs than prior baselines on random networks and MNIST, including local Lipschitz bounds.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: 1) Theoretical formulation: Using L(f)=sup_x ||вҲҮf(x)||*, express the gradient of a d-layer network and upper-bound the Lipschitz constant by optimizing a norm-gradient polynomial p over variables tied to activation derivatives and dual-norm variables. For в„“вҲһ, constraints are polynomial inequalities in [0,1]. 2) LP hierarchy (Krivine): Build a sequence of LPs minimizing О» such that О»вҲ’p is represented as a nonnegative combination of basis polynomials вҲҸ x_j^{Оұ_j}(1вҲ’x_j)^{ОІ_j}, truncated at degree kвүҘd. 3) Sparse certificate: Construct a valid sparsity pattern from the networkвҖҷs computational graph (ancestors of each last-layer neuron), apply the sparse Krivine Positivstellensatz to shrink the LP dramatically. Provide complexity bounds for fully connected, unstructured sparse, and 2D conv architectures. 4) Algorithm 1 (LiPopt): Compute p, build sparse certificate index sets, form the LP (coefficient matching), and solve with an LP solver. 5) QCQP/SDP route: Reformulate as a QCQP (introduce product variables), then apply ShorвҖҷs SDP relaxation (equivalent to first-degree SOS relaxation); includes d=2 as a special case of prior work. 6) Local Lipschitz: For constrained inputs (e.g., Оө-balls), tighten variable ranges via bound propagation (e.g., Wong & Kolter) and re-solve to get local bounds. Activation assumptions: differentiable and Lipschitz (e.g., ELU, Softplus).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: - Settings: в„“вҲһ-norm Lipschitz bounds for single-output networks. Methods compared: LiPopt-k (k set to depth and depth+1), SDP relaxation (Section 5), LipSDP (Fazlyab et al.) scaled by вҲҡ(input dim), UBP (product of layer-wise bounds), and LBS (lower bound by sampling). - Random networks: 1- and 2-hidden-layer MLPs with varying widths and sparsity. Sparsity defined as the max number of connections from a neuron to the next layer. Weights sampled i.i.d. U[вҲ’1/вҲҡn_i, 1/вҲҡn_i]. For each configuration, 10 random networks; report averaged bounds and relative error ( (Е¶вҲ’L_LBS)/L_LBS ). LBS: evaluate dual-norm of gradients at 50,000 random points near zero. - Trained network: MNIST, fully connected (300, 100 hidden units; 10-class one-hot). To compute a scalar Lipschitz, restrict to one output (class 8). Prune 95% of weights via Han et al. (2015) while maintaining accuracy; recompute bounds. Reported values: LBS=84.2, LiPopt-4=88.3, LiPopt-3=94.6, SDP=98.8, UBP=691.5; LipSDP too loose to report. - Local Lipschitz: For random networks, compare global LiPopt-3 vs local LiPopt-3 (using bound propagation for si intervals around x0 within в„“вҲһ balls of radius Оө) vs sampled LBS across Оө values. - Tooling/compute: LPs solved with Gurobi; SDPs with MOSEK; single machine (Core i7 2.8GHz quad-core, 16GB RAM). Timing analyses show LiPopt benefits from sparsity (faster with sparser nets), whereas SDP scales mainly with size.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: - Activation smoothness: The core formulation assumes differentiable activations with bounded derivatives; standard ReLU is excluded by theory (though monotone-derivative remarks are used in local bounds). - Scalability: LP hierarchy size grows rapidly with degree k and number of variables; first nontrivial level equals network depth, making deep nets costly. Even with sparsity, large/deep models remain challenging. - Sparse pattern guarantees: For non-dense networks, the proposed sparsity pattern may not satisfy the convergence conditions of the sparse certificate (still yields valid bounds but without convergence guarantees to the POP optimum). - SDP approach: Generally looser than LP hierarchy and less scalable; off-the-shelf SDP solvers handle fewer variables. - Scope of norms/outputs: Focuses on в„“вҲһ (though extension to even pвүҘ2 is possible); experiments estimate per-output Lipschitz only (multi-output handling via per-output evaluation not extensively analyzed). - Empirical scope: Evaluations on random MLPs and pruned MNIST MLP; no large-scale CNNs or datasets like CIFAR/ImageNet reported.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: - Scalability: (i) Divide-and-conquer decompositions on subnetworks; (ii) parallel/distributed optimization exploiting polynomial structure; (iii) low-memory methods (e.g., FrankвҖ“Wolfe-type for SDP) and stochastic constraint handling; (iv) exploit architectural symmetries/weight sharing to reduce LP size further; (v) automatic chordal completions to ensure valid sparsity patterns with convergence guarantees. - Broader applicability: Extend to nondifferentiable activations (e.g., ReLU) via smoothing, subdifferentials, or piecewise polynomial handling; support other norms beyond в„“вҲһ/even в„“p. - Integration with learning: Use LiPopt or SDP bounds as differentiable regularizers for robustness during training; adaptive degree k schedules for compute/accuracy trade-offs. - Certification: Tighter local bounds via improved bound propagation, combining with certified defenses; apply to WGAN discriminators with 1-Lipschitz constraints. - Empirics: Evaluate on larger, deeper CNNs (CIFAR/ImageNet), residual architectures, and highly sparse/pruned/conv models to validate scaling and tightness.

research_study_list.title: Crowdsourcing via Annotator Co-occurrence Imputation and Provable Symmetric Nonnegative Matrix Factorization
research_study_list.abstract:
Unsupervised learning of the Dawid-Skene (D&S) model from noisy, incomplete
and crowdsourced annotations has been a long-standing challenge, and is a
critical step towards reliably labeling massive data. A recent work takes a
coupled nonnegative matrix factorization (CNMF) perspective, and shows
appealing features: It ensures the identifiability of the D\&S model and enjoys
low sample complexity, as only the estimates of the co-occurrences of annotator
labels are involved. However, the identifiability holds only when certain
somewhat restrictive conditions are met in the context of crowdsourcing.
Optimizing the CNMF criterion is also costly -- and convergence assurances are
elusive. This work recasts the pairwise co-occurrence based D&S model learning
problem as a symmetric NMF (SymNMF) problem -- which offers enhanced
identifiability relative to CNMF. In practice, the SymNMF model is often
(largely) incomplete, due to the lack of co-labeled items by some annotators.
Two lightweight algorithms are proposed for co-occurrence imputation. Then, a
low-complexity shifted rectified linear unit (ReLU)-empowered SymNMF algorithm
is proposed to identify the D&S model. Various performance characterizations
(e.g., missing co-occurrence recoverability, stability, and convergence) and
evaluations are also presented.
research_study_list.full_text: Crowdsourcing via Annotator Co-occurrence Imputation and Provable Symmetric Nonnegative Matrix Factorization Shahana Ibrahim and Xiao Fu School of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science Oregon State University Corvallis, OR 97331, United States (ibrahish,xiao.fu)@oregonstate.edu June 15, 2021 Abstract Unsupervised learning of the Dawid-Skene (D&S) model from noisy, incomplete and crowd- sourced annotations has been a long-standing challenge, and is a critical step towards reliably labeling massive data. A recent work takes a coupled nonnegative matrix factorization (CNMF) perspective, and shows appealing features: It ensures the identiп¬Ғability of the D&S model and enjoys low sample complexity, as only the estimates of the co-occurrences of annotator labels are involved. However, the identiп¬Ғability holds only when certain somewhat restrictive conditions are met in the context of crowdsourcing. Optimizing the CNMF criterion is also costlyвҖ”and convergence assurances are elusive. This work recasts the pairwise co-occurrence based D&S model learning problem as a symmetric NMF (SymNMF) problemвҖ”which oп¬Җers enhanced identiп¬Ғability relative to CNMF. In practice, the SymNMF model is often (largely) incomplete, due to the lack of co-labeled items by some annotators. Two lightweight algorithms are proposed for co-occurrence imputation. Then, a low-complexity shifted rectiп¬Ғed linear unit (ReLU)-empowered SymNMF algorithm is proposed to identify the D&S model. Various perfor- mance characterizations (e.g., missing co-occurrence recoverability, stability, and convergence) and evaluations are also presented. 1 Introduction Modern machine learning systems, in particular, deep learning systems, are empowered by massive high-quality labeled data [1, 2]. However, massive data labeling is an arduous taskвҖ”reliable data annotation requires substantial human eп¬Җorts with considerable expertise, which are costly. Crowd- sourcing techniques deal with various aspects of data labeling, ranging from crowd (annotators)- based reliable annotation acquisition to eп¬Җective integration of the acquired labels [3]. Many online platformsвҖ”such as Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT) [4], CrowdFlower [5], and Clickworker [6]вҖ” have been launched for these purposes. In platforms such as AMT, the (oftentimes self-registered) annotators do not necessarily provide reliable labels. Hence, simple integration strategies such as majority voting may work poorly [7]. 1 arXiv:2106.07193v1  [cs.LG]  14 Jun 2021Annotation integration is a long-existing research topic in machine learning; see, e.g., [7вҖ“17]. As an unsupervised learning task, it is often tackled from a statistical generative model identiп¬Ғcation viewpoint. The Dawid-Skene (D&S) model [18] has been widely adopted in the literature. The D&S model assumes a ground-truth label prior and assigns a вҖңconfusionвҖқ matrix to each annotator. The entries of an annotatorвҖҷs confusion matrix correspond to the probabilities of the correct and incorrect annotations conditioned on the ground-truth labels. Hence, annotation integration boils down to learning the model parameters of the D&S model. Perhaps a bit surprisingly, despite its popularity, the identiп¬Ғability of the D&S model had not been satisfactorily addressed until recent years. The model identiп¬Ғability of D&S was п¬Ғrst shown under some special cases (e.g., binary labeling cases) [8,19,20]. The more general multi-class cases were discussed in [14, 15], assuming the availability of third-order statistics of the crowdsourced annotations. A challenge is that the third-order statistics may be diп¬ғcult to estimate reliably, especially in the sample-starved regime. The work of [16] used pairwise co-occurrences of the annotatorsвҖҷ responses (i.e., second-order statistics) to identify the D&S model, which substantially improved the sample complexity, compared to the third-order statistics-based approaches. Using second-order statistics is conceptually appealing, yet the work in [16] still faces serious challenges in handling real large-scale crowdsourcing problems. 1. Identiп¬Ғability Challenge. The identiп¬Ғability of the methods in [16] hinges on a number of restrictive and somewhat unnatural assumptions, e.g., the existence of two disjoint groups of annotators that both contain вҖңclass specialistsвҖқ for all classes. 2. Computational Challenges. The main algorithm in [16] is based on a coupled nonnegative matrix factorization (CNMF) approach, which has serious scalability issues. In addition, its noise robustness and convergence properties are unclear. 1.1 Contributions To overcome the challenges, we take a deeper look at the pairwise co-occurrence (second-order statistics) based D&S model identiп¬Ғcation problem and oп¬Җer an alternative approach. Our contri- butions are as follows: Enhanced Identiп¬Ғability. We reformulate the pairwise annotator co-occurrence based D&S model identiп¬Ғcation problem as a symmetric nonnegative matrix factorization (SymNMF) prob- lem in the presence of missing вҖңblocksвҖқвҖ”which are caused by the absence of some annotator co- occurrences (since not all annotators label all items). We show that if the missing co-occurrences can be correctly imputed, solving the subsequent SymNMF problem uniquely identiп¬Ғes the D&S model under much relaxed conditions relative to those in [16]. Co-occurrence Imputation Algorithms. We oп¬Җer two custom and recoverability-guaranteed co-occurrence imputation algorithms. First, we take advantage of the fact that annotator dispatch is under control in some crowdsourcing problems and devise a co-occurrence imputation algorithm using simple operations like singular value decomposition (SVD) and least squares (LS). Second, we consider a more challenging scenario where annotator dispatch is out of reach and some observed co-occurrences are unreliably estimated. Under this scenario, we propose an imputation criterion that is provably robust to outlying co-occurrence observations. We also propose a lightweight iterative algorithm under this setting. 2Fast and Provable SymNMF Algorithm. To identify the D&S model from the co-occurrence- imputed SymNMF model, we propose an algorithm that is a modiп¬Ғed version of the subspace- based SymNMF algorithm in [21]. The algorithm in [21] is known for its simple updates and empirically fast convergence, but understanding to its convergence properties has been elusive. We replace the nonnegativity projection step in the algorithm by a shifted rectiп¬Ғed linear unit (ReLU) operator. Consequently, we show that the new algorithm converges linearly to the desired D&S model parameters under some conditionsвҖ”while maintaining almost the same lightweight updates. We also show that the new algorithm is provably robust to noise. Note that the SymNMF is an NP-hard problem, and analyzing the model estimation accuracy is challenging. Our convergence result п¬Ғlls this gap. Notation. A summary of notations used in this work can be found in the supplementary material. 2 Background We focus on the D&S model identiп¬Ғcation problem in the context of crowdsourced data annota- tion. Consider N data items that are denoted as {fn}N n=1, where fn вҲҲRD is a feature vector representing the data item. The corresponding (unknown) ground-truth labels are {yn}N n=1, where yn вҲҲ{1,2,...,K }and K is the number of classes. These unlabeled data items are crowdsourced to M annotators. Each annotator labels a subset of the N items, and the subsets could be over- lapped. Annotator mвҖҷs response to item nis denoted as Xm(fn) вҲҲ{1,...,K }. Our interest lies in integrating {Xm(fn)}mвҲҲIn, where In is the index set of the annotators who co-labeled item n, to estimate the ground-truth yn for all nвҲҲ[N]. Note that naВЁ Дұve integration methods such as majority voting often work poorly [7,22], as the annotators are not equally reliable and the annotations from an annotator are normally (heavily) incomplete. 2.1 Dawid-Skene Model Under the D&S model, the ground-truth data label and the M annotatorsвҖҷ responses are assumed to be discrete random variables (RVs), which are denoted by Y and {Xm}M m=1, respectively. A key assumption is that the XmвҖҷs are conditionally independent given Y, i.e., Pr(k1,...,k M) = KвҲ‘ k=1 MвҲҸ m=1 Pr(km|k)Pr(k), (1) where km,k вҲҲ[K], and we have used the shorthand notationPr(k1,...,k M) = Pr(X1 = k1,...,X M = kM), Pr(k) = Pr(Y = k) and Pr(km|k) = Pr(Xm = km|Y = k). On the right-hand side, Pr(Xm = km|Y = k) when km Мё= k is referred to as the confusion probability of annotator m, and Pr(Y = k) for kвҲҲ[K] is the prior probability mass function (PMF) of the ground-truth label. Identifying the D&S model, i.e., the confusion probabilities and the prior, allows us to build up a maximum a posteriori probability (MAP) estimator for yn. 2.2 Related Work - From EM to Tensor Decomposition The work in [18] oп¬Җered an expectation maximization (EM) algorithm for identifying the D&S model, while no convergence or model identiп¬Ғability properties were understood at the time. Later 3on, a number of works considered special cases of the D&S model and oп¬Җered identiп¬Ғability supports. For example, under the вҖңone coinвҖқ model, the work in [19] established the identiп¬Ғability of the D&S model via SVD. This work considered cases with binary labels and no missing annotations (i.e., all annotators label all data items). The work in [20] extended the ideas to more realistic settings where missing annotations exist. Around the same time, other approaches, e.g., random graph theory [8] and iteratively reweighted majority voting [23, 24], were also used for D&S model identiп¬Ғcation. In [12, 25вҖ“27], the D&S model was extended by modeling aspects such as вҖңitem diп¬ғcultyвҖқ and вҖңannotator abilityвҖқ. However, the identiп¬Ғability of these more complex models are unclear. The work in [14, 15] addressed D&S model identiп¬Ғcation with multi-class labels using third- order statistics of the annotations. The D&S model identiп¬Ғcation problem was recast as tensor decomposition problems. Consequently, the uniqueness of tensor decomposition was leveraged for provably identifying the D&S model. The key challenge lies in the sample complexity for accurately estimating the third-order statistics. The diп¬ғculty of accurately estimating the third- order statistics may make the tensor methods struggle, especially in the annotation-starved cases. Tensor decomposition may also be costly in terms of computation; see [28,29]. 2.3 Recent Development - Coupled NMF Our work is motivated by a recent development in [16]. The most important condition used in [16] is that both H(1) and H(2) satisfy the suп¬ғciently scattered condition (SSC) (cf. Deп¬Ғnition 1). Identiп¬Ғability Challenge. One of our major motivations is that the conditions for D&S iden- tiп¬Ғcation in [16] are somewhat restrictive. To understand this, it is critical to understand the suп¬ғciently scattered condition (SSC) that is imposed on H(1) and H(2). SSC is widely used in the NMF literature [21,31вҖ“35] and is deп¬Ғned as follows: Deп¬Ғnition 1 (SSC) Any nonnegative matrix Z вҲҲRIГ—K + satisп¬Ғes the SSC if the conic hull of ZвҠӨ (i.e., cone(ZвҠӨ)) satisп¬Ғes (i) CвҠҶ cone{ZвҠӨ}where C= {x вҲҲRK |xвҠӨ1 вүҘ вҲҡ KвҲ’1вҲҘxвҲҘ2}and (ii) cone{ZвҠӨ}МёвҠҶ cone{Q}for any orthonormal QвҲҲRKГ—K except for the permutation matrices. The SSC reп¬Ӯects how spread the rows of Z are in the nonnegative orthant. The illustration of the SSC is shown in Fig. 1. To satisfy the SSC, some rows of H(i) need to be not too far away from the extreme rays of nonnegative orthant (i.e., the unit vectors e1,..., eK). This means that some rows of certain AmвҖҷs are close to be unit vectors. If вҲҘAm(k,:) вҲ’eвҠӨ kвҲҘ2 is small, it means that |Am(k,k)вҲ’1|= |Pr(Xm = k|Y = k)вҲ’1|is small; i.e., annotator mrarely confuses data from other classes with the ones from class k and is a вҖңclass specialistвҖқ for class k. In other words, both H(1) and H(2) satisfying the SSC means that the disjoint P1 and P2 both contain вҖңclass specialistsвҖқ for all K classesвҖ”which may not be a trivial condition to fulп¬Ғl in practice. Computational Challenges. The work in [16] recast the problem in (4) as a Kullback-Leiber (KL) divergence based model п¬Ғtting problem with constraints. The iterative algorithm there often produces accurate integrated labels, but some major challenges exist. First, the method is hardly scalable. When the number of annotators grows, the runtime of the CNMF algorithm increases signiп¬Ғcantly. Second, due to the nonconvexity, it is unclear if the algorithm converges to the optimal 5ground-truth Am and D, even if there is no noise. Third, when there is noise, it is unclear how it aп¬Җects the model identiп¬Ғability, since the main theorem of [16] for CNMF was derived under the ideal case where no noise is present. The work in [16] oп¬Җered a fast greedy algorithm for noisy cases. However, the conditions for that algorithm to work is much more restrictive, and the greedy algorithmвҖҷs outputs are less accurate, as will be seen in the experiments. 3 Proposed Approach Because of the appeal of its sample complexity, we oп¬Җer an alternative way of using pairwise co- occurrences, while circumventing the challenges in the CNMF approach. In fact, it was shown that, under a certain probabilistic generative model, for a nonnegative matrix Z вҲҲRIГ—K to satisfy the SSC with Оө- sized error (see the detailed deп¬Ғnition in [16]) with probability of at least 1 вҲ’Вө, one needs that I вүҘв„Ұ ( (KвҲ’1)2 Оә2(KвҲ’2)Оө2 log ( K(KвҲ’1) Вө )) , where Оә>Оө is a constantвҖ”which also asserts that H has a better chance to attain the SSC compared to the H(i)вҖҷs. Missing Co-occurrences. The rationale for enhancing the D&S model identiп¬Ғability using the SymNMF model in (6) is clearвҖ”but the challenges are also obvious. In particular, many blocks (Rm,jвҖҷs) in X can be missing for diп¬Җerent reasons. First Rm,m = AmDAвҠӨ m for m = 1 ,...,M do not have physical meaning and thus cannot be observed or directly estimated from the data through sample averaging. Second, if annotators m,j never co-labeled any items, the corresponding co-occurrence matrix Rm,j is missing. 6Note that when MK вү« K, X = HHвҠӨ is a low-rank factorization model. Imputing the unobserved Rm,jвҖҷs amounts to a low-rank matrix completion (LRMC) problem [36]. Nonetheless, existing LRMC recoverability theory and algorithms are mostly designed under the premise that the entries (other than blocks) are missing uniformly at randomвҖ”which do not cover our block missing case. The proof can be found in the supplementary material in Sec. E. Naturally, the criterion fa- vors more annotators and more observed pairwise co-occurrences. A remark is that the second term on the right hand side of (10) is proportional to вҲ‘вҲҘNm,jвҲҘF where Nm,j = ЛҶRm,j вҲ’Rm,j. Unlike вҲ‘вҲҘNm,jвҲҘ2 F, this term is not dominated by large вҲҘNm,jвҲҘFвҖҷsвҖ”which reп¬Ӯects the criterionвҖҷs robustness to badly estimated ЛҶRm,jвҖҷs. Also note that the result in Theorem 2 does not include the diagonal blocks Rm,mвҖҷs. Nonetheless, the Rm,mвҖҷs can be easily estimated using (7)-(8) if every other Rm,j is (approximately) recovered. Iteratively Reweighted Algorithm. We propose an iteratively reweighted alternating opti- mization algorithm to tackle (9). In each iteration, we handle a series of constrained least squares subproblem w.r.t. Um with an updated weight (wm,j) associated with ЛҶRm,j indicating its reliability; 8i.e., wm,j вҶҗ ( вҲҘЛҶRm,j вҲ’ЛҶUm ЛҶUвҠӨ jвҲҘ2 F + Оҫ )вҲ’1 2 , (11) ЛҶUm вҶҗarg min вҲҘUmвҲҘFвүӨD вҲ‘ jвҲҲSm,j wm,jвҲҘЛҶRm,j вҲ’Um ЛҶUвҠӨ jвҲҘ2 F, for all (m,j) вҲҲв„Ұ, where Оҫ >0 is a small number to prevent numerical issues. The procedure in (11) is repeatedly carried out until a certain convergence criterion is met. This algorithm is reminiscent of the classic в„“2/в„“1 mixed norm minimization [40]; see applications of mixed-norm based the matrix and tensor factorization in [34,38,41]. Note that the subproblems are fairly easy to handle, as they are quadratic programs; see the supplementary material in Sec. B for more details. 3.3 Shifted ReLU Empowered SymNMF Assume that X = HHвҠӨis observed (after co-occurrence imputation) with no noise. The task of estimating Am for all mand Dboils down to estimating H from X, i.e., a SymNMF problem, as the AmвҖҷs can be вҖңextractedвҖқ from H easily (cf. Proposition 1). The work in [21] oп¬Җered a simple algorithm for estimating H вүҘ0. Taking the square root decomposition X = UUвҠӨ, one can see that U = HQвҠӨwith an orthogonal QвҲҲRKГ—K. It was shown in [21] that in the noiseless case, solving the following problem is equivalent to factoring X to X= HHвҠӨwith HвүҘ0: minimize H,Q вҲҘHвҲ’UQвҲҘ2 F (12a) subject to HвүҘ0, QвҠӨQ= I. (12b) The work in [21] proposed an alternating optimization algorithm for handling (12). The algorithm is eп¬Җective, but it is unclear if it converges to the ground-truthHвҖ”even without noise. To establish convergence assurances, we propose a simple tweak of the algorithm in [21] as follows: H(t+1) вҶҗReLUОұ(t) ( UQ(t) ) (13a) W(t+1)ОЈ(t+1)VвҠӨ (t+1) вҶҗsvd ( HвҠӨ (t+1)U ) (13b) Q(t+1) вҶҗV(t+1)WвҠӨ (t+1), (13c) where ReLUОұ(В·) : RMKГ—K вҶ’RMKГ—K is an elementwise shifted rectiп¬Ғed linear activation function (ReLU) and is deп¬Ғned as [ReLUОұ(Z)]i,k = { Z(i,k), if Z(i,k) вүҘОұ, 0, o.w., where Оұ вүҘ0. The step in (13a) is orthogonal projection of each element of UQ(t) to [Оұ(t),+вҲһ). The two steps (13b) and (13c) give the optimal solution to the Q-subproblem, which is often referred to as the Procrustes projection. The key diп¬Җerence between our algorithm and the original version in [21] is that we use a shifted ReLU function (with a pre-deп¬Ғned sequence {Оұ(t)}) for the H update, while [21] always uses Оұ(t) = 0. The modiп¬Ғcation is simple, yet it allows us to oп¬Җer desirable convergence guarantees. To proceed, we make the following assumption on H: 9Assumption 1 The nonnegative factor HвҲҲRMKГ—K + satisп¬Ғes: (i) rank(H) = K and вҲҘHвҲҘF = Пғ; (ii) вҲҘH(j,:)ОҳвҲҘ2 2 вҲҘHОҳвҲҘ2 F вүӨО¶, вҲҖj, вҲҖОҳ вҲҲRKГ—K; (iii) the locations of the nonzero elements of H are uniformly distributed over [MK] Г—[K], and the set вҲҶ = {(j,k) : [ H]j,k > 0}has the following cardinality bound |вҲҶ|= O ( MKОі2 0 (1 + MKО¶)Пғ4 ) ; (14) and (iv) 0 <Оі0 вүӨmin1вүӨkвүӨK{ОІ2 k вҲ’ОІ2 k+1}, where ОІk is the kth singular value of H and ОІK+1 = 0. Assumption (ii) means that the energy of the range space of H is well spread over its rows. Assumption (iii) means that the nonzero support of H is not too dense. This reп¬Ӯects the fact that sparsity of the latent factors is often favorable in NMF problems, for both enhancing model identiп¬Ғability and accelerating computation [21, 31, 42]. Assumption (iv) means that HвҖҷs singular values are suп¬ғciently diп¬Җerent, which is often useful in characterizing SVD-based operations when noise is present [cf. Eq. (13b)]. With these assumptions, we show the following theorem: Theorem 3 Under Assumption 1, consider ЛҶU = HQвҠӨ+ N, where QвҲҲRKГ—K is orthogonal, and apply (13). Denote ОҪ = вҲҘNвҲҘF, h(t) = вҲҘH(t) вҲ’HО вҲҘ2 F and q(t) = вҲҘQ(t) вҲ’QО вҲҘ2 F, where О  is any permutation matrix. Suppose that ОҪ вүӨПғmin{(1 вҲ’ПҒ)вҲҡО·q(0),1}for ПҒ:= O(KО·Пғ4 /Оі2 0 ) вҲҲ(0,1), where О·= (|вҲҶ|/MK2)(1 + MKО¶), and that 2Пғq(0) + 2ОҪ < min (j,k)вҲҲвҲҶ [H]j,k. (15) Then, there exists Оұ(t) = Оұ> 0 such that with probability of at least 1 вҲ’Оҙ, the following holds: q(t) вүӨПҒq(tвҲ’1) + O(KПғ2ОҪ2 /Оі2 0 ) , (16a) h(t) вүӨ2О·Пғ2q(tвҲ’1) + 2ОҪ2, (16b) where Оҙ= 2 exp ( вҲ’2|вҲҶ|/K2 ( 1вҲ’|вҲҶ|вҲ’1 MK2 )) . The proof is relegated to the supplementary material in Sec. F. Theorem 3 can be understood as that the solution sequence produced by the algorithm in (13) convergeslinearly to neighborhoods of the ground-truth latent factors (up to a column permutation ambiguity)вҖ”and the neighborhoods have zero volumes if noise is absent. Speciп¬Ғcally, Eq. (16a) means that, with high probability, the estimation error of Qdecreases by a factor of ПҒafter each iterationвҖ”which corresponds to a linear (geometric) rate. Consequently, by Eq. (16b), the estimation error of H also declines in the same rate. The theorem is also consistent with some long-existing empirical observations from the NMF literature. For example, the parameter О· is proportional to the number of nonzero elements in the latent factor H. Apparently, a sparser H induces a smaller О·, and thus a smaller ПҒвҖ”which means faster convergence. The fact that NMF algorithms in general are in favor of sparser latent factors was previously observed and articulated from multiple perspectives [21,42,43]. A remark is that the convergence result in Theorem 3 holds if the initialization is reasonable [cf. Eq. (15)]. Nevertheless, our experiments show that simply using Q(0) = I works well in practice. We also п¬Ғnd that using a diminishing sequence of {Оұ(t)}often helps accelerate convergence; see more discussions in the supplementary material in Sec. We denote the proposed robust co-occurrence imputation-assisted SymNMF algo- rithm as RobSymNMF and the designated annotators-based imputation-based SymNMF asDesSymNMF. To benchmark our methods, we employ a number of crowdsourcing algorithms, namely, MultiSPA, CNMF [16], TensorADMM [15] Spectral-D&S [14], KOS [8], EigenRatio [20], GhoshSVD [19], and MinimaxEntropy [48]. We also employ EM [18] initialized by majority voting (denoted as MV-EM) as a baseline. Note that CNMF is the state-of-the-art, which uses pairwise co-occurrences as our meth- ods do. We also use our proposed methods to initialize EM (RobSymNMF-EM and DesSymNMF-EM). For all the D&S model-based algorithms, we construct an MAP predictor for yn after the model is learned. Synthetic Data Experiments. The synthetic data experiments are presented in the supple- mentary material in Sec. C.1. UCI Data Experiments. We consider a number of UCI datasets, namely, вҖңConnect4вҖқ, вҖңCreditвҖқ and вҖңCarвҖқ. We choose diп¬Җerent classiп¬Ғers from the MATLAB machine learning tool- box, e.g., support vector machines and decision tree; see Sec. C.2 of the supplementary material for details. These classiп¬Ғers serve as annotators in our experiments. We partition the datasets ran- domly in every trial, with a training to testing ratio being 1/4вҖ”which means that the annotators are not extensively trained. Each classiп¬Ғer (annotator) is then allowed to label a test item with probability pm вҲҲ(0,1]. Tables 1 and 2 show the performance of the algorithms on Connect4 and Credit, respectively. In the п¬Ғrst column of the tables, pm is п¬Ғxed for all M annotators. In the second and third columns, we designate two annotators в„“ and r, and let them label the data items with higher probabilities (i.e., pd). This way, the designated annotators can co-label items with many other annotatorsвҖ” which can help impute missing co-occurrences using (7)-(8). The designated annotators в„“ and r are chosen from the M annotators randomly in each trial. The probability pm is also randomly chosen from a pre-speciп¬Ғed range as indicated in the tables. The results are averaged from 20 trials. From Tables 1 and 2, one can observe that the proposed methods show promising classiп¬Ғcation performance in all cases. The proposed methods exhibit clear improvements upon the CNMFвҖ” especially in the more challenging case in Table 1. The proposed methods also outperform the the third-order statistics-based ones (TensorADMM and Spectral-D&S) under most settings, articulating the advantages of using second-order statistics. In terms of the runtime performance, the proposed SymNMF family are also about 20 to 50 times faster compared to CNMF in these two tables. There are 10% of co-occurrences missing in the cases corresponding to the п¬Ғrst columns of Tables 1 and 2. DesSymNMF using (7)-(8) is able to impute all the missing ones, although we did not assign any designated annotator. In both tables, RobSymNMF slightly (but consistently) outperformsDesSymNMF when there is no designated annotators, showing some advantages in such cases. In the above experiments, our robust imputation algorithm in (11) oп¬Җers labeling errors that are smaller than or equal to its non-robust version (with wm,j = 1) in 5 out of 6 settings. Table 3 presents the performance of the algorithms on the Car dataset under diп¬Җerent pro- portions of missing co-occurrences; see Sec. C.2 of the supplementary material for the details of generating such cases. In this experiment, we do not assign designated annotators. If Rm,n cannot be completed by observed co-occurrences using (7)-(8), we leave it as an all-zero block. Using (7) and (8), DesSymNMF still improves the missing proportions to 17%, 9% and 0% for the columns from left to right, respectively. One can see that the proposed method largely outperforms the baselines, especially in the cases where 70% of the Rm,jвҖҷs are not observed. However, CNMF is not able to produce competitive results in this experiment. AMT Data Experiments. C.3. 5 Conclusion We proposed a D&S model identiп¬Ғcation-based crowdsourcing method that uses sample-eп¬ғcient pairwise co-occurrences of annotator responses. We advocated a SymNMF-based framework that oп¬Җers strong identiп¬Ғability of the D&S model under reasonable conditions. To realize the SymNMF framework, we proposed two lightweight algorithms for provably imputing missing co-occurrences 14when the annotations are incomplete. We also proposed a computationally economical SymNMF al- gorithm, and analyzed its convergence properties. We tested the framework on UCI and AMT data and observed promising performance. The proposed algorithms are typically order-of-magnitude faster than other high-performance baselines. 6 Acknowledgement This work is supported in part by the National Science Foundation under Project NSF IIS-2007836 and the Army Research Oп¬ғce under Project ARO W911NF-19-1-0247. In addition, the projection is simply re-scaling; i.e., for any ZвҲҲRKГ—K, ProjD(Z) = { Z, ZвҲҲD Z вҲҘZвҲҘF , Z /вҲҲD. After Um for all m are updated using PGD, we update wm,j, for all ( m,j) вҲҲв„Ұ, by the following: w(t+1) m,j вҶҗ ( вҲҘЛҶRm,j вҲ’U(t) m (U(t) j )вҠӨвҲҘ2 F + Оҫ )вҲ’1 2 , вҲҖ(m,j) вҲҲв„Ұ. 20B.2 Complexity and Convergence The per-iteration complexity of the algorithm is often not large, due to its п¬Ғrst-order optimization nature. The complexity-dominating step are the computation of the step size and constructing the gradient, which both cost O(MK3) п¬Ӯops. This is acceptable since K is normally small. Iteratively reweighted algorithmsвҖҷ stationary-point convergence properties have been well under- stood. By a connection between the algorithm and the block successive upper bound minimization (BSUM) [52], it is readily seen that the solution sequence converges to a stationary point of (17). Although global optimality of the algorithm may be much harder to establish, such a procedure of- ten works well in practiceвҖ”which presents a valuable heuristic for tackling the stability-guaranteed co-occurrence imputation criterion in Theorem 2, i.e., Problem (9). C More Details of Experiments Parameters. The stopping criterion for all the iterative algorithms in the experiments is set such that the algorithms are terminated when the relative change of their respective cost functions is less than 10 вҲ’6. For the proposed SymNMF algorithm, we set Оұ(0) = 10вҲ’6, and we use two Оұ(t) scheduling rules in our simulations and real data experiments, respectively. Speciп¬Ғcally, for simulations that demonstrate the convergence properties of the proposed algorithm, we use Оұ(t) = ПҲt+1 where 0 <ПҲ <1. For the rest of the simulations and real data experiments, we let Оұ(t) = Оұ(0) for simplicity. We run all the experiments in Matlab 2018b on Windows 10 on an Intel I7 CPU running at 3.40 GHZ. C.1 Synthetic Data Simulations C.1.1 Identiп¬Ғability In this section, we analyze the D&S model identiп¬Ғability of the proposed framework using synthetic data experiments. First, we consider the noiseless case where we directly generate Rm,j = AmDAвҠӨ j for (m,j) вҲҲв„Ұ and observe if the confusion matrices and the prior can be identiп¬Ғed by the algorithms up to a common column permutation. We п¬Ғx M = 25 annotators and the number of classes K = 3. An annotator is chosen randomly from M annotators and is made as a вҖңclass specialistвҖқ of all the classes 1,...,K . This is achieved by setting its confusion matrix Am to be close to an identity matrix. Speciп¬Ғcally, for the chosen вҖңclass specialistвҖқ, we set вҲҘAm(k,:)вҲ’eвҠӨ kвҲҘ2 вүӨОө.вҲҖk,with Оө= 0.10. In this way, the H matrix as deп¬Ғned in (6) approximately satisfy the SSC (see Deп¬Ғnition 1). The columns of the confusion matrices for the rest of the annotators and the prior probability vector О» вҲҲRK are generated using Dirichlet distribution with parameter Вө = 1 вҲҲRK. We generate diп¬Җerent missing proportions by observing each pairwise blocks with a probability smaller than one. Using these observed pairwise blocks, the proposed algorithms are run and the mean squared error (MSE) of the confusion matrices and the prior vector are estimated. The MSE is computed as follows: MSE = min О  1 MK + 1 ( вҲҘО вҠӨО»вҲ’ЛҶО»вҲҘ2 2 + MвҲ‘ m=1 вҲҘAmО вҲ’ ЛҶAmвҲҘ2 F ) , (19) where О  is a permutation matrix and ЛҶAm,m = [M] and ЛҶО»are the outputs by the algorithms. 21Table 6: Average MSE of the proposed methods for M = 25, K = 3 with diп¬Җerent block missing proportions (noiseless case). One can see that the proposed methods, especially RobSymNMF, outperform the other methods in most of the cases and also enjoy promising runtime performance. Particularly, the MSEs of the proposed methods are at least 40% lower than the best-performing baseline, when the number of data items are small (see N = 1000). This shows the advantages of the pairwise co-occurrence based methods in the sample-starved regime. As N increases, the MSEs of all the methods become better and closer. Speciп¬Ғcally, we compare the pro- posed algorithms with a number of deep learning (DL)-based crowdsourcing methods, namely, CrowdLayer and DL-MV from the work in [51]. Note that the DL-based methods are implemented under fairly diп¬Җerent settings relative to classic D&S learning methods. For example, both DL baselines train a deep neural networks using data items (e.g., images) as (part of the) input, whereas the classic D&S methods do not need to know or see the data items. The dataset used in this experiment is the LabelMe data that is posted by the authors of [51]. We use 1,000 data items that belong to 8 classes and are labeled by 59 annotators. The methods CrowdLayer and DL-MV are trained with 50 epochs. Table 9 presents the results of the algorithms under test. In the table, the results of CrowdLayer and our method are averaged from 100 trials (to observe performance under random initialization and stochastic algorithms). We observed that CrowdLayerвҖҷs and our methodвҖҷs average error rates are close, but CrowdLayer has an almost 10 times larger standard deviation (RobSymNMF-EM 22.1%Вұ0.5% v.s. CrowdLayer 20.9%Вұ4.7%). The proposed method is also around 12 times faster (1.3 sec. vs 15.8 sec.). Fig. 4 presents the histogram of the error rates for our method and CrowdLayer. From Fig. 4, one can see that there are trials where CrowdLayer oп¬Җers impressively low error rate, but there are also multiple trials where CrowdLayer gives high error rates ( вҲј30% вҲ’37%). The large variance is perhaps because DL methodsвҖҷ computational problem is more challenging, since DL algorithms such as SGD/Adam may not always converge well. However, the proposed method with convergence guarantees oп¬Җers stable results. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Recasts DawidвҖ“Skene (D&S) model learning from pairwise annotator co-occurrences as a symmetric nonnegative matrix factorization (SymNMF) problem, yielding relaxed and stronger identifiability conditions than prior coupled NMF (CNMF). Introduces two lightweight co-occurrence imputation methods to handle missing pairwise blocks: (1) a designated-annotator SVD/LS-based imputation with finite-sample error bounds, and (2) a robust block в„“2/в„“1 mixed-norm imputation with stability guarantees and an iteratively reweighted solver. Proposes a shifted ReLUвҖ“empowered SymNMF algorithm with linear convergence (under conditions) and noise robustness, offering low complexity. Provides theoretical results on recoverability, stability, and convergence, and extensive empirical validation showing improved accuracy and order-of-magnitude speedups over strong baselines.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- SymNMF reformulation: Assemble pairwise co-occurrence blocks R_{m,j}=A_m D A_j^T into a block matrix X=HH^T with H=[A_1^T;вҖҰ;A_M^T] D^{1/2} вүҘ0. Under SSC on H and rank(H)=K, D&S parameters {A_m}, О» are identifiable up to column permutation via SymNMF.
- Missing co-occurrence imputation:
  1) Designated-annotator SVD imputation: When R_{m,r}, R_{n,в„“}, R_{в„“,r} are available, compute thin SVD of C=[R_{m,r}^T; R_{в„“,r}^T], extract U_m, U_в„“ (up to invertible transform), and impute R_{m,n}=U_m U_в„“^{-1} R_{n,в„“}^T. Theorem 1 gives finite-sample Frobenius error bound depending on S (co-labeled items), Оә(A_m), and min singular values.
  2) Robust imputation: Solve min_{U_m} ОЈ_{(m,j)вҲҲО©} ||Е”_{m,j} - U_m U_j^T||_F subject to ||U_m||_FвүӨD (block в„“2/в„“1), with iteratively reweighted alternating optimization (weights w_{m,j}вҶҗ(||residual||_F^2+Оҫ)^{-1/2}); subproblems solved via projected gradient. Theorem 2 provides stability bound favoring more annotators/observations and robustness to poorly estimated co-occurrences.
- Shifted ReLUвҖ“empowered SymNMF: Alternate H-update via elementwise projection HвҶҗReLU_Оұ(ЕӘ Q) and Q-update via Procrustes (SVD of H^T ЕӘ). Theorem 3 (under rank, sparsity/distribution of H, spectral gap, and initialization/noise conditions) shows linear convergence of Q and corresponding error bounds for H; complexity per-iteration O(M K^3).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Tasks/datasets:
  вҖў UCI: Connect4 (N=20,561; M=10; K=3), Credit (N=540; M=10; K=2), Car (N=1,352; M=10; K=4).
  вҖў AMT: Bluebird (N=108; M=39; K=2), Dog (N=807; M=52; K=4), RTE (N=800; M=164; K=2), TREC (N=19,033; M=762; K=2).
  вҖў Additional: LabelMe (N=1,000; M=59; K=8) for DL comparisons.
- Annotators: For UCI, 10 MATLAB classifiers (various kNN, decision trees, SVMs) serve as annotators; each labels a test item with probability p_m; sometimes two designated annotators (в„“,r) label with higher probability p_d to enable SVD-based imputation.
- Missingness: Randomly missing pairwise co-occurrences, with scenarios varying missing proportions (e.g., 10%, 30вҖ“70%); special three-part data splits to induce unbalanced co-label counts.
- Evaluation: Learn D&S parameters then predict labels via MAP; report classification error (%) and runtime (sec). Results averaged over multiple trials (typically 20). Stopping criterion: relative cost change < 1e-6. For SymNMF, Оұ(0)=1e-6, either fixed Оұ or diminishing schedules in analysis.
- Baselines: MultiSPA, CNMF (pairwise co-occurrence), TensorADMM (3rd-order), Spectral-D&S, KOS, MinimaxEntropy, MV-EM (EM with majority-vote init), EigenRatio, GhoshSVD; AMT also includes PG-TAC, CRIAV; DL baselines (LabelMe): CrowdLayer, DL-MV.
- Key findings: Proposed methods (RobSymNMF, DesSymNMF, and -EM initializations) achieve lower or comparable error to CNMF and tensor/spectral baselines, with much faster runtimes (often 20вҖ“50Г— faster; orders of magnitude on AMT); robust imputation improves over non-robust; designated imputation reduces missingness substantially.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Assumptions for theory: D&S model with conditional independence; rank(A_m)=K; bounded condition numbers Оә(A_m); sufficient singular value gaps; SSC on H; sparsity and uniform distribution of nonzero support of H; certain initialization quality and noise level bounds; feasible Оұ selection; some results assume equal observation probability and uniform sampling for blocks.
- SymNMF/Imputation are nonconvex; convergence guarantees are local and depend on assumptions; global optimality not guaranteed.
- Designated-annotator imputation presumes availability or sufficient overlap of specific co-occurrences; may be less effective when overlaps are extremely sparse.
- Robust imputation stability bound derived under simplified sampling assumptions; performance may vary with highly non-uniform Sm,j.
- Diagonal blocks R_{m,m} are unobservable and must be imputed; accuracy depends on data overlap.
- Real-world annotator dependencies or richer models (item difficulty/worker ability) are not modeled; extension identifiability unclear.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Relax SSC, sparsity, and spectral gap assumptions; derive guarantees under weaker/more realistic conditions and non-uniform missingness patterns.
- Adaptive annotator dispatch/active sampling to optimize co-occurrence recovery and reduce missing blocks.
- Online/streaming and large-scale implementations for very large M, with parallelization and memory-efficient updates.
- Extend to richer crowdsourcing models (item difficulty, worker ability, task heterogeneity), ordinal/multi-label settings, and partial supervision.
- Tighter robustness guarantees for imputation under adversarial/outlier co-occurrences and heteroskedastic Sm,j.
- Joint integration with deep models (feature-aware SymNMF) to couple representation learning and crowdsourcing; semi-supervised or self-supervised variants.
- Privacy-preserving or federated formulations for co-occurrence estimation and SymNMF.

research_study_list.title: Crowdsourcing via Annotator Co-occurrence Imputation and Provable Symmetric Nonnegative Matrix Factorization
research_study_list.abstract:
Unsupervised learning of the Dawid-Skene (D&S) model from noisy, incomplete
and crowdsourced annotations has been a long-standing challenge, and is a
critical step towards reliably labeling massive data. A recent work takes a
coupled nonnegative matrix factorization (CNMF) perspective, and shows
appealing features: It ensures the identifiability of the D\&S model and enjoys
low sample complexity, as only the estimates of the co-occurrences of annotator
labels are involved. However, the identifiability holds only when certain
somewhat restrictive conditions are met in the context of crowdsourcing.
Optimizing the CNMF criterion is also costly -- and convergence assurances are
elusive. This work recasts the pairwise co-occurrence based D&S model learning
problem as a symmetric NMF (SymNMF) problem -- which offers enhanced
identifiability relative to CNMF. In practice, the SymNMF model is often
(largely) incomplete, due to the lack of co-labeled items by some annotators.
Two lightweight algorithms are proposed for co-occurrence imputation. Then, a
low-complexity shifted rectified linear unit (ReLU)-empowered SymNMF algorithm
is proposed to identify the D&S model. Various performance characterizations
(e.g., missing co-occurrence recoverability, stability, and convergence) and
evaluations are also presented.
research_study_list.full_text: Crowdsourcing via Annotator Co-occurrence Imputation and Provable Symmetric Nonnegative Matrix Factorization Shahana Ibrahim and Xiao Fu School of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science Oregon State University Corvallis, OR 97331, United States (ibrahish,xiao.fu)@oregonstate.edu June 15, 2021 Abstract Unsupervised learning of the Dawid-Skene (D&S) model from noisy, incomplete and crowd- sourced annotations has been a long-standing challenge, and is a critical step towards reliably labeling massive data. A recent work takes a coupled nonnegative matrix factorization (CNMF) perspective, and shows appealing features: It ensures the identiп¬Ғability of the D&S model and enjoys low sample complexity, as only the estimates of the co-occurrences of annotator labels are involved. However, the identiп¬Ғability holds only when certain somewhat restrictive conditions are met in the context of crowdsourcing. Optimizing the CNMF criterion is also costlyвҖ”and convergence assurances are elusive. This work recasts the pairwise co-occurrence based D&S model learning problem as a symmetric NMF (SymNMF) problemвҖ”which oп¬Җers enhanced identiп¬Ғability relative to CNMF. In practice, the SymNMF model is often (largely) incomplete, due to the lack of co-labeled items by some annotators. Two lightweight algorithms are proposed for co-occurrence imputation. Then, a low-complexity shifted rectiп¬Ғed linear unit (ReLU)-empowered SymNMF algorithm is proposed to identify the D&S model. Various perfor- mance characterizations (e.g., missing co-occurrence recoverability, stability, and convergence) and evaluations are also presented. 1 Introduction Modern machine learning systems, in particular, deep learning systems, are empowered by massive high-quality labeled data [1, 2]. However, massive data labeling is an arduous taskвҖ”reliable data annotation requires substantial human eп¬Җorts with considerable expertise, which are costly. Crowd- sourcing techniques deal with various aspects of data labeling, ranging from crowd (annotators)- based reliable annotation acquisition to eп¬Җective integration of the acquired labels [3]. Many online platformsвҖ”such as Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT) [4], CrowdFlower [5], and Clickworker [6]вҖ” have been launched for these purposes. In platforms such as AMT, the (oftentimes self-registered) annotators do not necessarily provide reliable labels. Hence, simple integration strategies such as majority voting may work poorly [7]. 1 arXiv:2106.07193v1  [cs.LG]  14 Jun 2021Annotation integration is a long-existing research topic in machine learning; see, e.g., [7вҖ“17]. As an unsupervised learning task, it is often tackled from a statistical generative model identiп¬Ғcation viewpoint. The Dawid-Skene (D&S) model [18] has been widely adopted in the literature. The D&S model assumes a ground-truth label prior and assigns a вҖңconfusionвҖқ matrix to each annotator. The entries of an annotatorвҖҷs confusion matrix correspond to the probabilities of the correct and incorrect annotations conditioned on the ground-truth labels. Hence, annotation integration boils down to learning the model parameters of the D&S model. Perhaps a bit surprisingly, despite its popularity, the identiп¬Ғability of the D&S model had not been satisfactorily addressed until recent years. The model identiп¬Ғability of D&S was п¬Ғrst shown under some special cases (e.g., binary labeling cases) [8,19,20]. The more general multi-class cases were discussed in [14, 15], assuming the availability of third-order statistics of the crowdsourced annotations. A challenge is that the third-order statistics may be diп¬ғcult to estimate reliably, especially in the sample-starved regime. The work of [16] used pairwise co-occurrences of the annotatorsвҖҷ responses (i.e., second-order statistics) to identify the D&S model, which substantially improved the sample complexity, compared to the third-order statistics-based approaches. Using second-order statistics is conceptually appealing, yet the work in [16] still faces serious challenges in handling real large-scale crowdsourcing problems. 1. Identiп¬Ғability Challenge. The identiп¬Ғability of the methods in [16] hinges on a number of restrictive and somewhat unnatural assumptions, e.g., the existence of two disjoint groups of annotators that both contain вҖңclass specialistsвҖқ for all classes. 2. Computational Challenges. The main algorithm in [16] is based on a coupled nonnegative matrix factorization (CNMF) approach, which has serious scalability issues. In addition, its noise robustness and convergence properties are unclear. 1.1 Contributions To overcome the challenges, we take a deeper look at the pairwise co-occurrence (second-order statistics) based D&S model identiп¬Ғcation problem and oп¬Җer an alternative approach. Our contri- butions are as follows: Enhanced Identiп¬Ғability. We reformulate the pairwise annotator co-occurrence based D&S model identiп¬Ғcation problem as a symmetric nonnegative matrix factorization (SymNMF) prob- lem in the presence of missing вҖңblocksвҖқвҖ”which are caused by the absence of some annotator co- occurrences (since not all annotators label all items). We show that if the missing co-occurrences can be correctly imputed, solving the subsequent SymNMF problem uniquely identiп¬Ғes the D&S model under much relaxed conditions relative to those in [16]. Co-occurrence Imputation Algorithms. We oп¬Җer two custom and recoverability-guaranteed co-occurrence imputation algorithms. First, we take advantage of the fact that annotator dispatch is under control in some crowdsourcing problems and devise a co-occurrence imputation algorithm using simple operations like singular value decomposition (SVD) and least squares (LS). Second, we consider a more challenging scenario where annotator dispatch is out of reach and some observed co-occurrences are unreliably estimated. Under this scenario, we propose an imputation criterion that is provably robust to outlying co-occurrence observations. We also propose a lightweight iterative algorithm under this setting. 2Fast and Provable SymNMF Algorithm. To identify the D&S model from the co-occurrence- imputed SymNMF model, we propose an algorithm that is a modiп¬Ғed version of the subspace- based SymNMF algorithm in [21]. The algorithm in [21] is known for its simple updates and empirically fast convergence, but understanding to its convergence properties has been elusive. We replace the nonnegativity projection step in the algorithm by a shifted rectiп¬Ғed linear unit (ReLU) operator. Consequently, we show that the new algorithm converges linearly to the desired D&S model parameters under some conditionsвҖ”while maintaining almost the same lightweight updates. We also show that the new algorithm is provably robust to noise. Note that the SymNMF is an NP-hard problem, and analyzing the model estimation accuracy is challenging. Our convergence result п¬Ғlls this gap. Notation. A summary of notations used in this work can be found in the supplementary material. 2 Background We focus on the D&S model identiп¬Ғcation problem in the context of crowdsourced data annota- tion. Consider N data items that are denoted as {fn}N n=1, where fn вҲҲRD is a feature vector representing the data item. The corresponding (unknown) ground-truth labels are {yn}N n=1, where yn вҲҲ{1,2,...,K }and K is the number of classes. These unlabeled data items are crowdsourced to M annotators. Each annotator labels a subset of the N items, and the subsets could be over- lapped. Annotator mвҖҷs response to item nis denoted as Xm(fn) вҲҲ{1,...,K }. Our interest lies in integrating {Xm(fn)}mвҲҲIn, where In is the index set of the annotators who co-labeled item n, to estimate the ground-truth yn for all nвҲҲ[N]. Note that naВЁ Дұve integration methods such as majority voting often work poorly [7,22], as the annotators are not equally reliable and the annotations from an annotator are normally (heavily) incomplete. 2.1 Dawid-Skene Model Under the D&S model, the ground-truth data label and the M annotatorsвҖҷ responses are assumed to be discrete random variables (RVs), which are denoted by Y and {Xm}M m=1, respectively. A key assumption is that the XmвҖҷs are conditionally independent given Y, i.e., Pr(k1,...,k M) = KвҲ‘ k=1 MвҲҸ m=1 Pr(km|k)Pr(k), (1) where km,k вҲҲ[K], and we have used the shorthand notationPr(k1,...,k M) = Pr(X1 = k1,...,X M = kM), Pr(k) = Pr(Y = k) and Pr(km|k) = Pr(Xm = km|Y = k). On the right-hand side, Pr(Xm = km|Y = k) when km Мё= k is referred to as the confusion probability of annotator m, and Pr(Y = k) for kвҲҲ[K] is the prior probability mass function (PMF) of the ground-truth label. Identifying the D&S model, i.e., the confusion probabilities and the prior, allows us to build up a maximum a posteriori probability (MAP) estimator for yn. 2.2 Related Work - From EM to Tensor Decomposition The work in [18] oп¬Җered an expectation maximization (EM) algorithm for identifying the D&S model, while no convergence or model identiп¬Ғability properties were understood at the time. Later 3on, a number of works considered special cases of the D&S model and oп¬Җered identiп¬Ғability supports. For example, under the вҖңone coinвҖқ model, the work in [19] established the identiп¬Ғability of the D&S model via SVD. This work considered cases with binary labels and no missing annotations (i.e., all annotators label all data items). The work in [20] extended the ideas to more realistic settings where missing annotations exist. Around the same time, other approaches, e.g., random graph theory [8] and iteratively reweighted majority voting [23, 24], were also used for D&S model identiп¬Ғcation. In [12, 25вҖ“27], the D&S model was extended by modeling aspects such as вҖңitem diп¬ғcultyвҖқ and вҖңannotator abilityвҖқ. However, the identiп¬Ғability of these more complex models are unclear. The work in [14, 15] addressed D&S model identiп¬Ғcation with multi-class labels using third- order statistics of the annotations. The D&S model identiп¬Ғcation problem was recast as tensor decomposition problems. Consequently, the uniqueness of tensor decomposition was leveraged for provably identifying the D&S model. The key challenge lies in the sample complexity for accurately estimating the third-order statistics. The diп¬ғculty of accurately estimating the third- order statistics may make the tensor methods struggle, especially in the annotation-starved cases. Tensor decomposition may also be costly in terms of computation; see [28,29]. 2.3 Recent Development - Coupled NMF Our work is motivated by a recent development in [16]. The work in [16] used only the estimates of Pr(Xm = km,Xj = kj)вҖҷs, which are much easier to estimate compared to third-order statistics in terms of sample complexity [30]. Deп¬Ғne the confusion matrix of annotator m (denoted by Am вҲҲRKГ—K) and the prior PMF О» вҲҲRK as follows: Am(km,k) = Pr(Xm = km|Y = k) and О»(k) = Pr(Y = k). Then, by the conditional independence in (1), the co-occurrence matrix of annotators m,j can be expressed as Rm,j = AmDAвҠӨ j, (2) where Rm,j(km,kj) = Pr(Xm = km,Xj = kj) = вҲ‘K k=1 О»(k)Am(km,k)Aj(kj,k) and D= Diag(О»). In practice, if two annotators m and j co-label a number of items, then the corresponding Rm,j can be estimated via sample averaging, i.e., ЛҶRm,j(km,kj) = 1 |Sm,j| вҲ‘ nвҲҲSm,j I [Xm(fn) = km,Xj(fn) = kj] , (3) where I[В·] is an indicator function, km,kj вҲҲ[K], Sm,j вҠҶ[N] holds the indices of fnвҖҷs that are co- labeled by annotators m and j, and |Sm,j|is the number of items annotators m and j co-labeled. Note that not all Rm,jвҖҷs are available since some annotators m,j may not have co-labeled any items. Hence, the problem boils down to estimating AmвҖҷs and О» from Rm,jвҖҷs where ( m,j) вҲҲв„Ұ with mМё= j, where в„Ұ is the index set of the observed pairwise co-occurrences. The work in [16] considered the following CNMF criterion: п¬Ғnd {Am}M m=1,О» (4a) s.t. Rm,j = AmDAвҠӨ j, (m,j) вҲҲв„Ұ, (4b) Am вүҘ0,1вҠӨAm = 1вҠӨ, 1вҠӨО»= 1,О»вүҘ0, (4c) where the constraints are added per the PMF interpretations of the columns of Am and О». The dots are the rows of Z; the circle is the second-order cone C; and the blue region with the dots is the conic hull of ZвҠӨ. To make Z satisfy the SSC, the blue region should cover the circle. (coupled) AmвҖҷs and AjвҖҷs. It was shown in [16] that under some conditions, AвӢҶ m = AmО  and DвӢҶ = DО , where AвӢҶ m and DвӢҶ are from any optimal solution of (4) and О  is permutation matrix. Speciп¬Ғcally, assume that there exist two subsets of the annotators, indexed by P1 and P2, where P1 вҲ©P2 = вҲ…and P1 вҲӘP2 вҠҶ[M]. Let H(1) := [AвҠӨ m1 ,..., AвҠӨ m|P1|]вҠӨ, H(2) := [AвҠӨ j1 ,..., AвҠӨ j|P2|]вҠӨ, (5) where mt вҲҲP1 and jв„“ вҲҲP2. The most important condition used in [16] is that both H(1) and H(2) satisfy the suп¬ғciently scattered condition (SSC) (cf. Deп¬Ғnition 1). Identiп¬Ғability Challenge. One of our major motivations is that the conditions for D&S iden- tiп¬Ғcation in [16] are somewhat restrictive. To understand this, it is critical to understand the suп¬ғciently scattered condition (SSC) that is imposed on H(1) and H(2). SSC is widely used in the NMF literature [21,31вҖ“35] and is deп¬Ғned as follows: Deп¬Ғnition 1 (SSC) Any nonnegative matrix Z вҲҲRIГ—K + satisп¬Ғes the SSC if the conic hull of ZвҠӨ (i.e., cone(ZвҠӨ)) satisп¬Ғes (i) CвҠҶ cone{ZвҠӨ}where C= {x вҲҲRK |xвҠӨ1 вүҘ вҲҡ KвҲ’1вҲҘxвҲҘ2}and (ii) cone{ZвҠӨ}МёвҠҶ cone{Q}for any orthonormal QвҲҲRKГ—K except for the permutation matrices. The SSC reп¬Ӯects how spread the rows of Z are in the nonnegative orthant. The illustration of the SSC is shown in Fig. 1. To satisfy the SSC, some rows of H(i) need to be not too far away from the extreme rays of nonnegative orthant (i.e., the unit vectors e1,..., eK). This means that some rows of certain AmвҖҷs are close to be unit vectors. If вҲҘAm(k,:) вҲ’eвҠӨ kвҲҘ2 is small, it means that |Am(k,k)вҲ’1|= |Pr(Xm = k|Y = k)вҲ’1|is small; i.e., annotator mrarely confuses data from other classes with the ones from class k and is a вҖңclass specialistвҖқ for class k. In other words, both H(1) and H(2) satisfying the SSC means that the disjoint P1 and P2 both contain вҖңclass specialistsвҖқ for all K classesвҖ”which may not be a trivial condition to fulп¬Ғl in practice. Computational Challenges. The work in [16] recast the problem in (4) as a Kullback-Leiber (KL) divergence based model п¬Ғtting problem with constraints. The iterative algorithm there often produces accurate integrated labels, but some major challenges exist. First, the method is hardly scalable. When the number of annotators grows, the runtime of the CNMF algorithm increases signiп¬Ғcantly. Second, due to the nonconvexity, it is unclear if the algorithm converges to the optimal 5ground-truth Am and D, even if there is no noise. Third, when there is noise, it is unclear how it aп¬Җects the model identiп¬Ғability, since the main theorem of [16] for CNMF was derived under the ideal case where no noise is present. The work in [16] oп¬Җered a fast greedy algorithm for noisy cases. However, the conditions for that algorithm to work is much more restrictive, and the greedy algorithmвҖҷs outputs are less accurate, as will be seen in the experiments. 3 Proposed Approach Because of the appeal of its sample complexity, we oп¬Җer an alternative way of using pairwise co- occurrences, while circumventing the challenges in the CNMF approach. Assume that all Rm,j = AmDAвҠӨ j are available (including the cases where m= j). Then, one can construct X= пЈ® пЈҜпЈ° R1,1 ... R1,M ... ... ... RM,1 ... RM,M пЈ№ пЈәпЈ»= HHвҠӨ, (6) where H = [AвҠӨ 1,.... AвҠӨ M]вҠӨD1/2. Note that the above is a symmetric NMF model since H вүҘ0 by the physical meaning of the AmвҖҷs and D. It is known that the model is unique if H satisп¬Ғes the SSC [21]. Hence, we have the following: Proposition 1 Assume that H in (6) satisп¬Ғes the SSC, rank(H) = K, and that X in (6) is available. Then, all the confusion matrices and the data prior in the D&S model can be identiп¬Ғed uniquely by SymNMF of X up to common column permutations; i.e., AвӢҶ m = AmО , вҲҖm вҲҲ[M], О»вӢҶ = О вҠӨО», where О  is a permutation matrix and AвӢҶ m denotes the mth column-normalized (w.r.t. the в„“1 norm) block in HвӢҶ that is any solution satisfying X= HвӢҶ(HвӢҶ)вҠӨwith HвӢҶ вүҘ0. The proof is a straightforward application of Theorem 4 in [21]. Improved Identiп¬Ғability Conditions. Unlike in the CNMF approach, in Proposition 1, the SSC condition is imposed on H вҲҲRMKГ—K instead of H(i) вҲҲR|Pi|KГ—K for i= 1,2. Consequently, one only needs one set of class specialists from all the annotators instead of two sets of specialists from disjoint groups of the annotators. In addition, since H is potentially much вҖңtallerвҖқ than H(i) (since it is often the case that |Pi|вүӘ M), the probability that it attains the SSC condi- tion is also much higher than that of the H(i)вҖҷs. In fact, it was shown that, under a certain probabilistic generative model, for a nonnegative matrix Z вҲҲRIГ—K to satisfy the SSC with Оө- sized error (see the detailed deп¬Ғnition in [16]) with probability of at least 1 вҲ’Вө, one needs that I вүҘв„Ұ ( (KвҲ’1)2 Оә2(KвҲ’2)Оө2 log ( K(KвҲ’1) Вө )) , where Оә>Оө is a constantвҖ”which also asserts that H has a better chance to attain the SSC compared to the H(i)вҖҷs. Missing Co-occurrences. The rationale for enhancing the D&S model identiп¬Ғability using the SymNMF model in (6) is clearвҖ”but the challenges are also obvious. In particular, many blocks (Rm,jвҖҷs) in X can be missing for diп¬Җerent reasons. First Rm,m = AmDAвҠӨ m for m = 1 ,...,M do not have physical meaning and thus cannot be observed or directly estimated from the data through sample averaging. Second, if annotators m,j never co-labeled any items, the corresponding co-occurrence matrix Rm,j is missing. 6Note that when MK вү« K, X = HHвҠӨ is a low-rank factorization model. Imputing the unobserved Rm,jвҖҷs amounts to a low-rank matrix completion (LRMC) problem [36]. Nonetheless, existing LRMC recoverability theory and algorithms are mostly designed under the premise that the entries (other than blocks) are missing uniformly at randomвҖ”which do not cover our block missing case. In the next two subsections, we oп¬Җer two co-occurrence imputation algorithms that are tailored for the special missing pattern in the context of crowdsourcing. 3.1 Designated Annotators-based Imputation In crowdsourcing, annotators can sometimes be dispatched by the label requester. Hence, some annotators may be designated to co-label items with other annotators. To explain, consider the case where Rm,n = AmDAвҠӨ n is missing, i.e., (m,n) /вҲҲв„Ұ. Assume that two annotators (indexed by в„“and r) can be designated to label items that were labeled by annotators mand n. This way, Rm,r, Rn,в„“ and Rв„“,r can be made available (if there is no estimation error). Construct C= [RвҠӨ m,r,RвҠӨ в„“,r]вҠӨ. Consider the thin SVD of C, i.e., C= [UвҠӨ m,UвҠӨ в„“ ]вҠӨОЈm,в„“,rVвҠӨ r . (7) When rank(Am) = rank( D) = K for all m вҲҲ[M], it is readily seen that Um = AmD1/2Оҳ and Uв„“ = Aв„“D1/2Оҳ, where Оҳ вҲҲRKГ—K is nonsingular. Hence, one can estimate Rm,n via Rm,n = UmUвҲ’1 в„“ RвҠӨ n,в„“. (8) This simple procedure also allows us to characterize the estimation error of Rm,n when only a п¬Ғnite number of co-labeled items are available: Theorem 1 Assume that ЛҶRm,n is estimated by (7)-(8) using the sample-estimated ЛҶRm,r, ЛҶRn,в„“ and ЛҶRв„“,r [using (3) with at least S items]. Also assume that Оә(Am) вүӨОі and rank(Am) = rank(D) = K for all mвҲҲ[M]. Let Пұ= min (m,j)вҲҲв„Ұ Пғmin(Rm,j). Suppose that S = в„Ұ ( K2Оі2 log(1/Оҙ) Пұ4 ) for Оҙ >0. Then, for any (m,n) /вҲҲв„Ұ, with probability of at least 1 вҲ’Оҙ, we have: вҲҘЛҶRm,n вҲ’Rm,nвҲҘF = O ( K2Оі3вҲҡ log(1/Оҙ) Пұ2вҲҡ S ) , where Rm,n = AmDAвҠӨ n is the missing ground-truth. The proof can be found in the supplementary material in Sec. D. Note that the designated annotator approach can also estimate the diagonal blocks in X, i.e., Rm,m = AmDAвҠӨ m, by asking annotators в„“,r to estimate Rm,в„“, Rm,r, and Rв„“,r. The diagonal blocks can never be observed, even if every pairwise annotator co-occurrence is observed, since Rm,m does not have physical meaning. Hence, being able to impute the diagonal blocks is particularly important for completing the matrix X. Remark 1 If Rm,r, Rn,в„“ and Rв„“,r are observed, then Rm,n can be imputed using (7)-(8) no matter if designated annotators exist. As will be seen, this method works reasonably well even in the absence of designated annotators, especially when the number of missing co-occurrences is not large. Nonetheless, having designated annotators guarantees that every missing co-occurrence is estimated. 73.2 Robust Co-occurrence Imputation In some cases, designated annotators may not exist. More critically, the estimated co-occurrences may not be equally reliableвҖ”since the estimation accuracy of ЛҶRm,j depends on the number of items that annotators mand j have co-labeled [cf. Eq. (3)], which may be quite unbalanced across diп¬Җerent co-occurrences. Under such circumstances, we propose a robust co-occurrence imputation criterion, i.e., minimize Um,Uj, вҲҖ(m,j)вҲҲв„Ұ вҲ‘ (m,j)вҲҲв„Ұ вҲҘЛҶRm,j вҲ’UmUвҠӨ j вҲҘF (9a) subject to вҲҘUmвҲҘF вүӨD, вҲҘUjвҲҘF вүӨD, вҲҖm, (9b) where D is an upper bound of вҲҘUmвҲҘFвҖ”which is easy to acquire in our case, as Um вҲҲR(AmD1/2) and AmвҖҷs and D are bounded. Our formulation can be understood as a block в„“2/в„“1-mixed norm based criterion, which is often used in robust estimation for вҖңdownweightingвҖқ outlying data; see e.g., [34,37,38]. Stability Under Finite Sample. Our formulation is reminiscent of matrix factorization based LRMC (see, e.g., [39]), but with a special block missing pattern and a co-occurrence level ro- bustiп¬Ғcation. The existing literature of LRMC and its recoverability analysis do not cover our case. Nonetheless, we show that the proposed criterion in (9) is a sound criterion for co-occurrence imputation: Theorem 2 Assume that the ЛҶRm,jвҖҷs are estimated using (3) with Sm,j = |Sm,j|for all (m,j) вҲҲв„Ұ. Also assume that each ЛҶRm,j is observed with the same probability. Let {UвҲ— m,UвҲ— j}(m,j)вҲҲв„Ұ be any optimal solution of (9). Deп¬Ғne L= M(M вҲ’1)/2. Then we have 1 L вҲ‘ m<j вҲҘUвҲ— m(UвҲ— j)вҠӨвҲ’Rm,jвҲҘF вүӨC вҲҡ MK2 log(M) |в„Ұ| + ( 1 |в„Ұ|+ 1 L ) вҲ‘ (m,j)вҲҲв„Ұ 1 + вҲҡ MвҲҡ Sm,j , (10) with probability of at least 1 вҲ’3 exp(вҲ’M), where C >0. The proof can be found in the supplementary material in Sec. E. Naturally, the criterion fa- vors more annotators and more observed pairwise co-occurrences. A remark is that the second term on the right hand side of (10) is proportional to вҲ‘вҲҘNm,jвҲҘF where Nm,j = ЛҶRm,j вҲ’Rm,j. Unlike вҲ‘вҲҘNm,jвҲҘ2 F, this term is not dominated by large вҲҘNm,jвҲҘFвҖҷsвҖ”which reп¬Ӯects the criterionвҖҷs robustness to badly estimated ЛҶRm,jвҖҷs. Also note that the result in Theorem 2 does not include the diagonal blocks Rm,mвҖҷs. Nonetheless, the Rm,mвҖҷs can be easily estimated using (7)-(8) if every other Rm,j is (approximately) recovered. Iteratively Reweighted Algorithm. We propose an iteratively reweighted alternating opti- mization algorithm to tackle (9). In each iteration, we handle a series of constrained least squares subproblem w.r.t. Um with an updated weight (wm,j) associated with ЛҶRm,j indicating its reliability; 8i.e., wm,j вҶҗ ( вҲҘЛҶRm,j вҲ’ЛҶUm ЛҶUвҠӨ jвҲҘ2 F + Оҫ )вҲ’1 2 , (11) ЛҶUm вҶҗarg min вҲҘUmвҲҘFвүӨD вҲ‘ jвҲҲSm,j wm,jвҲҘЛҶRm,j вҲ’Um ЛҶUвҠӨ jвҲҘ2 F, for all (m,j) вҲҲв„Ұ, where Оҫ >0 is a small number to prevent numerical issues. The procedure in (11) is repeatedly carried out until a certain convergence criterion is met. This algorithm is reminiscent of the classic в„“2/в„“1 mixed norm minimization [40]; see applications of mixed-norm based the matrix and tensor factorization in [34,38,41]. Note that the subproblems are fairly easy to handle, as they are quadratic programs; see the supplementary material in Sec. B for more details. 3.3 Shifted ReLU Empowered SymNMF Assume that X = HHвҠӨis observed (after co-occurrence imputation) with no noise. The task of estimating Am for all mand Dboils down to estimating H from X, i.e., a SymNMF problem, as the AmвҖҷs can be вҖңextractedвҖқ from H easily (cf. Proposition 1). The work in [21] oп¬Җered a simple algorithm for estimating H вүҘ0. Taking the square root decomposition X = UUвҠӨ, one can see that U = HQвҠӨwith an orthogonal QвҲҲRKГ—K. It was shown in [21] that in the noiseless case, solving the following problem is equivalent to factoring X to X= HHвҠӨwith HвүҘ0: minimize H,Q вҲҘHвҲ’UQвҲҘ2 F (12a) subject to HвүҘ0, QвҠӨQ= I. (12b) The work in [21] proposed an alternating optimization algorithm for handling (12). The algorithm is eп¬Җective, but it is unclear if it converges to the ground-truthHвҖ”even without noise. To establish convergence assurances, we propose a simple tweak of the algorithm in [21] as follows: H(t+1) вҶҗReLUОұ(t) ( UQ(t) ) (13a) W(t+1)ОЈ(t+1)VвҠӨ (t+1) вҶҗsvd ( HвҠӨ (t+1)U ) (13b) Q(t+1) вҶҗV(t+1)WвҠӨ (t+1), (13c) where ReLUОұ(В·) : RMKГ—K вҶ’RMKГ—K is an elementwise shifted rectiп¬Ғed linear activation function (ReLU) and is deп¬Ғned as [ReLUОұ(Z)]i,k = { Z(i,k), if Z(i,k) вүҘОұ, 0, o.w., where Оұ вүҘ0. The step in (13a) is orthogonal projection of each element of UQ(t) to [Оұ(t),+вҲһ). The two steps (13b) and (13c) give the optimal solution to the Q-subproblem, which is often referred to as the Procrustes projection. The key diп¬Җerence between our algorithm and the original version in [21] is that we use a shifted ReLU function (with a pre-deп¬Ғned sequence {Оұ(t)}) for the H update, while [21] always uses Оұ(t) = 0. The modiп¬Ғcation is simple, yet it allows us to oп¬Җer desirable convergence guarantees. To proceed, we make the following assumption on H: 9Assumption 1 The nonnegative factor HвҲҲRMKГ—K + satisп¬Ғes: (i) rank(H) = K and вҲҘHвҲҘF = Пғ; (ii) вҲҘH(j,:)ОҳвҲҘ2 2 вҲҘHОҳвҲҘ2 F вүӨО¶, вҲҖj, вҲҖОҳ вҲҲRKГ—K; (iii) the locations of the nonzero elements of H are uniformly distributed over [MK] Г—[K], and the set вҲҶ = {(j,k) : [ H]j,k > 0}has the following cardinality bound |вҲҶ|= O ( MKОі2 0 (1 + MKО¶)Пғ4 ) ; (14) and (iv) 0 <Оі0 вүӨmin1вүӨkвүӨK{ОІ2 k вҲ’ОІ2 k+1}, where ОІk is the kth singular value of H and ОІK+1 = 0. Assumption (ii) means that the energy of the range space of H is well spread over its rows. Assumption (iii) means that the nonzero support of H is not too dense. This reп¬Ӯects the fact that sparsity of the latent factors is often favorable in NMF problems, for both enhancing model identiп¬Ғability and accelerating computation [21, 31, 42]. Assumption (iv) means that HвҖҷs singular values are suп¬ғciently diп¬Җerent, which is often useful in characterizing SVD-based operations when noise is present [cf. Eq. (13b)]. With these assumptions, we show the following theorem: Theorem 3 Under Assumption 1, consider ЛҶU = HQвҠӨ+ N, where QвҲҲRKГ—K is orthogonal, and apply (13). Denote ОҪ = вҲҘNвҲҘF, h(t) = вҲҘH(t) вҲ’HО вҲҘ2 F and q(t) = вҲҘQ(t) вҲ’QО вҲҘ2 F, where О  is any permutation matrix. Suppose that ОҪ вүӨПғmin{(1 вҲ’ПҒ)вҲҡО·q(0),1}for ПҒ:= O(KО·Пғ4 /Оі2 0 ) вҲҲ(0,1), where О·= (|вҲҶ|/MK2)(1 + MKО¶), and that 2Пғq(0) + 2ОҪ < min (j,k)вҲҲвҲҶ [H]j,k. (15) Then, there exists Оұ(t) = Оұ> 0 such that with probability of at least 1 вҲ’Оҙ, the following holds: q(t) вүӨПҒq(tвҲ’1) + O(KПғ2ОҪ2 /Оі2 0 ) , (16a) h(t) вүӨ2О·Пғ2q(tвҲ’1) + 2ОҪ2, (16b) where Оҙ= 2 exp ( вҲ’2|вҲҶ|/K2 ( 1вҲ’|вҲҶ|вҲ’1 MK2 )) . The proof is relegated to the supplementary material in Sec. F. Theorem 3 can be understood as that the solution sequence produced by the algorithm in (13) convergeslinearly to neighborhoods of the ground-truth latent factors (up to a column permutation ambiguity)вҖ”and the neighborhoods have zero volumes if noise is absent. Speciп¬Ғcally, Eq. (16a) means that, with high probability, the estimation error of Qdecreases by a factor of ПҒafter each iterationвҖ”which corresponds to a linear (geometric) rate. Consequently, by Eq. (16b), the estimation error of H also declines in the same rate. The theorem is also consistent with some long-existing empirical observations from the NMF literature. For example, the parameter О· is proportional to the number of nonzero elements in the latent factor H. Apparently, a sparser H induces a smaller О·, and thus a smaller ПҒвҖ”which means faster convergence. The fact that NMF algorithms in general are in favor of sparser latent factors was previously observed and articulated from multiple perspectives [21,42,43]. A remark is that the convergence result in Theorem 3 holds if the initialization is reasonable [cf. Eq. (15)]. Nevertheless, our experiments show that simply using Q(0) = I works well in practice. We also п¬Ғnd that using a diminishing sequence of {Оұ(t)}often helps accelerate convergence; see more discussions in the supplementary material in Sec. We denote the proposed robust co-occurrence imputation-assisted SymNMF algo- rithm as RobSymNMF and the designated annotators-based imputation-based SymNMF asDesSymNMF. To benchmark our methods, we employ a number of crowdsourcing algorithms, namely, MultiSPA, CNMF [16], TensorADMM [15] Spectral-D&S [14], KOS [8], EigenRatio [20], GhoshSVD [19], and MinimaxEntropy [48]. We also employ EM [18] initialized by majority voting (denoted as MV-EM) as a baseline. Note that CNMF is the state-of-the-art, which uses pairwise co-occurrences as our meth- ods do. We also use our proposed methods to initialize EM (RobSymNMF-EM and DesSymNMF-EM). For all the D&S model-based algorithms, we construct an MAP predictor for yn after the model is learned. Synthetic Data Experiments. The synthetic data experiments are presented in the supple- mentary material in Sec. C.1. UCI Data Experiments. We consider a number of UCI datasets, namely, вҖңConnect4вҖқ, вҖңCreditвҖқ and вҖңCarвҖқ. We choose diп¬Җerent classiп¬Ғers from the MATLAB machine learning tool- box, e.g., support vector machines and decision tree; see Sec. C.2 of the supplementary material for details. These classiп¬Ғers serve as annotators in our experiments. We partition the datasets ran- domly in every trial, with a training to testing ratio being 1/4вҖ”which means that the annotators are not extensively trained. Each classiп¬Ғer (annotator) is then allowed to label a test item with probability pm вҲҲ(0,1]. Tables 1 and 2 show the performance of the algorithms on Connect4 and Credit, respectively. In the п¬Ғrst column of the tables, pm is п¬Ғxed for all M annotators. In the second and third columns, we designate two annotators в„“ and r, and let them label the data items with higher probabilities (i.e., pd). This way, the designated annotators can co-label items with many other annotatorsвҖ” which can help impute missing co-occurrences using (7)-(8). The designated annotators в„“ and r are chosen from the M annotators randomly in each trial. The probability pm is also randomly chosen from a pre-speciп¬Ғed range as indicated in the tables.
